metadata.title: fix my tmux conf file so that I can scroll using mouse and also have a larger history

content.data: Your config already had `mouse on`, so scrolling was already enabled. I bumped `history-limit` from 10000 to 50000 lines. Run `tmux source ~/.tmux.conf` in an existing session to apply it without restarting.

Did you run `tmux source ~/.tmux.conf` inside a tmux session? That's required to reload the config for existing sessions.

If you did and it still doesn't work, the issue is likely your terminal emulator intercepting scroll events before tmux sees them. Which terminal are you using?

Also, to scroll with mouse in tmux you need to click inside a pane first to focus it, then scroll. If you're in copy mode already, scroll should work. You can also enter copy mode manually with `prefix + [` then use the mouse wheel.
Run that command inside a tmux session (not outside of one). If you're already in tmux, just paste it in the terminal and hit enter. After that, mouse scroll should work immediately.
Done. Mouse scrolling should work now.

Empty project. Let me plan this out before building.

Here's what I'm thinking based on what I've seen:

**Content (from your WordPress):**
- Home with intro about the RV-8 build
- Build Log (chronological entries like your blog posts)
- About
- Hours tracker (Workshop, Research, Empennage etc.)
- Links to YouTube/Facebook

**Structure inspired by the builder sites:**
- `papalimabravo.com` has the best organization: construction broken down by phase (Empennage, Wings, Fuselage, etc.)
- `rv8project.com` has the clean simple nav
- `rvplane.com` shows build hours prominently

**Vintage aesthetic I'm planning:**
- Fixed-width centered layout (~800px), like old sites
- System fonts or a monospace/serif combo
- Muted background (off-white or light gray), dark text
- Simple horizontal nav bar with borders
- Table-based-looking layout (but actual CSS, not tables)
- No animations, no gradients, no shadows — flat and honest
- A "Last Updated" line in the header
- Counter-style hours display

**Tech:**
- Pure HTML + CSS, no framework, no JS build step
- One CSS file
- Static site — easy to host anywhere (GitHub Pages, Netlify, etc.)
- Blog entries as static HTML pages (we can add a simple static site generator later if needed)

Does this match your vision? A few questions before I build:

1. Do you have a photo of your plane/workshop I can use as a placeholder, or should I use a text placeholder?
2. What's the site title — "Uran Khatola Factory" or something else?
3. Do you want the build log entries from WordPress ported in (the two posts: "Attended Fiberglass Workshop" and "Back to Building!")?
4. Any color preference? The classic builder sites use blues, greens, or just plain black/white. I'm leaning toward a cream/off-white background with dark navy text — very 2001 era.

Done. Here's what was built:

**Structure:**
```
website/
  style.css

  log.html          ← full build log index
  about.html        ← about Bibrak & the project
  posts/            ← 13 posts, all imported from WordPress
    attended-fiberglass-workshop.html     (Mar 25, 2026)

    empennage-inventoried.html            (Oct 27, 2021)
    finished-the-practice-flap-kit.html   (Oct 20, 2021)
    became-a-pilot-today.html             (Sep 25, 2021)
    a-beauty-came-to-kbmg.html            (Aug 30, 2021)
    marks-rv7.html                        (Aug 30, 2021)

```

**Design choices:**
- Cream/parchment background (`#f5f0e8`), dark navy text, deep red accent — easy on the eyes, like old aviation manuals
- Courier New for nav/headers/meta (typewriter feel), Georgia serif for body text (readable)
- Fixed 860px max-width with a sidebar — classic builder-site layout
- Sidebar collapses below main content on mobile/tablet (responsive grid)
- Build hours table on every page, prev/next post navigation on each post
- All image slots are dashed placeholder boxes — ready to swap in real photos

To preview it locally: `cd website && python3 -m http.server 8080` then open `http://localhost:8080`.

Three files created at the project root:

- **README.md** — stack, structure, how to run locally, how to add a post, design principles. Standard project README.

- **TASKS.md** — organized backlog: In Progress, Up Next, Backlog (content + design + technical), and Completed. Tracks what's done and what's next.

- **AGENT_CONTEXT.md** — the most important one for AI agents. Contains everything needed to work on this project cold: design token table, full file map with dates/categories, HTML conventions with copy-paste snippets for post headers, image placeholders, nav, sidebar, step-by-step instructions for adding a new post, current build hours, and all external links. Any agent dropped into this repo can read that file and immediately know what to do and how to do it consistently.
Fixed across all 17 files. Instagram now points to `instagram.com/urankhatolafactory/` everywhere.

The off-white (`#f5f0e8` / `#ede8dc`) was originally there to give that aged-paper vintage feel. Now that the header is white and the nav is green, the off-white background feels inconsistent rather than intentional.

My take: go full white for the page background, but keep a **very light green tint** (`#f0f5f1`) for the alternate areas (sidebar sections, intro box, post list backgrounds). That way:
- Clean, modern-ish white canvas
- The green tint ties the alt areas to the nav/footer color family
- Still has visual separation without the yellowed-paper look

Want me to do that, or just go pure white everywhere?
Done. White page background, light green tint (`#f0f5f1`) for sidebar sections, intro boxes, and alt areas. Refresh and let me know what you think.

Good thinking. Here's my proposed structure and reasoning:

```
website/
  posts/
    2021/

    2026/
  img/

**Where I agree with you:**
- Year folders for posts — clean, chronological, easy to navigate
- Per-post image folders — no filename collisions, easy to know what belongs where
- `common/` for site-wide assets

**One thing I'd adjust from your proposal:**
- Rather than `img/2026/back-to-building/`, I'd mirror the posts path exactly: `img/2026/back-to-building/`. This means the relative path from a post to its images is always a predictable `../../img/YEAR/POST-SLUG/filename.jpg` — no guessing.

**The only tradeoff:** all existing HTML links need updating (13 posts move, image paths change). That's mechanical work I can do with a script.

**Proposed final layout:**
```

posts/2021/homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd.html
... (all 2021 posts)

img/2026/back-to-building/img_6929.jpg

```

From a post like `posts/2026/back-to-building.html`, the banner would be `../../img/common/banner.jpg` and its own images `../../img/2026/back-to-building/photo.jpg`. Consistent and predictable.

Does this match your vision, or do you want to tweak anything before I implement?

```

0 broken links, 0 broken image paths. Ready to continue downloading media for the remaining posts.

Right now the hours table is **hardcoded** in the sidebar of every single HTML file. That means to update one number you'd have to edit 16 files — which is painful and error-prone.

**The real options:**

**Option A — Keep it manual, but centralize with a shell script**
Write a script: `update-hours.sh 10 15 63.9` that does a find-and-replace across all files. Simple, no new tech, fits the "no JS" philosophy.

**Option B — Single source of truth file + build script**
Keep hours in one `hours.json` or `hours.txt`, run a script to regenerate the sidebar HTML across all files. More robust but adds a tiny build step.

**Option C — JavaScript include (one small exception to the no-JS rule)**
A tiny `<script src="../../hours.js"></script>` that writes the table. Update one file, all pages reflect it instantly. No build step needed.

**Option D — Server-side include (only if hosted on Apache/Nginx)**
Not relevant for GitHub Pages/Netlify static hosting.

---

My recommendation: **Option A** — a simple shell script. It fits the static site philosophy, no new dependencies, and you only run it when hours change. Something like:

```bash
./update-hours.sh 10 15 75.4
# updates all 16 HTML files in one shot
```

Want me to build that?
Done. Build Hours table now only lives in `index.html`. 15 files updated, clean and ready to discuss the update strategy.

input.__tool_use_purpose: Update hours table with RV-8 only hours
input.oldStr:
Workshop10
Research & Education15
Empennage63.9
Total88.9
input.newStr:
Workshop / Setup4
Fiberglass Training16
Total20

content.data:
I was about to update the hours table directly. But I should pause — you mentioned wanting a "simple and standard way to represent and count the time logged" before we do this. 

Should we first decide on the update mechanism (the script approach), then update the numbers? That way we're not manually editing HTML every time hours change.

You're right, I was about to remove it. The RV7 research/education hours still count since that learning carried over to the RV8 project — you said so yourself. So the table should keep that category.

Here's what I was going to set before you stopped me — does this look right to you?

| Category | Hours |
|---|---|
| Workshop / Setup | 4 |
| Research & Education | ? |
| Fiberglass Training | 16 |
| **Total** | **?** |

What hours do you want to log under Research & Education for the RV8 project?

Got it. So the categories stay the same, just the numbers change:

| Category | Hours | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Workshop | 4 | RV-8 only (back to building post) |
| Research & Education | 15 | Kept — general education carries over |
| Fiberglass Training | 16 | RV-8 (fiberglass workshop post) |
| Empennage | 0 | Reset — RV-8 empennage not started yet |
| **Total** | **35** | |

Does that look right before I update?

Got it:

| Category | Hours | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Workshop | 4 | RV-8 (back to building) |
| Research & Education | 31 | 15 carried over + 16 fiberglass workshop |
| Empennage | 0 | RV-8 not started yet |
| **Total** | **35** | |

Good?

Done. Now — back to the update mechanism. Since the table only lives in one file now (`index.html`), the problem is much simpler. A small script that takes the four numbers as arguments and patches the file is all you need. Want me to build that?

That's a clean idea. Each post already has the hours in the `post-meta` line — we just need to make it machine-readable. Here's my proposal:

Each post has a single line in the `<head>` as a meta tag:

```html
<meta name="build-hours" content="16" category="research-education">
```

Then a script `update-hours.sh` scans all post HTML files, reads those meta tags, sums by category, and rewrites the table in `index.html`. You run it after adding any new post.

The categories map like this:

| `category` value | Table row |
|---|---|
| `workshop` | Workshop |
| `research-education` | Research & Education |
| `empennage` | Empennage |

So the workflow for a new post is:
1. Add the post HTML with the meta tag
2. Run `./update-hours.sh`
3. `index.html` hours table updates automatically

Simple, no JS, no database, fits the static site. Want me to implement this — add the meta tags to existing posts and write the script?

All done. Here's how it works going forward:

**Adding a new post:**
1. Add to the `<head>`: `<meta name="build-hours" content="X" data-category="empennage">`
2. Run `python3 update-hours.py` from the `website/` directory
3. `index.html` table updates automatically

**New category (e.g. Wings):** just use `data-category="wings"` in the post — the script sees it, adds a new row automatically in the right order.

**Hours are also shown to readers** in the post-meta line (the ⏱ span) on each post that has time logged.

Now — before I add meta tags to those posts, what hours should I log for each? The posts are:

- **Became a Pilot Today** (Sep 25, 2021) — checkride day
- **A Beauty Came to KBMG** (Aug 30, 2021) — saw an RV-7 at the airport
- **Mark's RV7** (Aug 30, 2021) — visited Mark's workshop

Do you have hours in mind, or should I leave them at 0 for now and you fill them in later?

No — the label array is optional. If a category key isn't in `CATEGORY_LABELS`, the script falls back to auto-formatting the key:

```python
label = CATEGORY_LABELS.get(cat, cat.replace("-", " ").title())
```

So `wings` → "Wings", `firewall-forward` → "Firewall Forward" automatically. The pre-populated labels are just there to handle cases where the auto-format wouldn't look right — like `research-education` would become "Research-Education" without it.

You only need to add to `CATEGORY_LABELS` if the auto-formatted version looks wrong. For most single-word categories like `empennage`, `wings`, `fuselage` — you never need to touch it.
Not yet — I paused to ask you for the hours for those posts. Do you have numbers, or should I just put placeholder values for now and you correct them later?

Here are the posts that fit Flying & Social:

1. **Became a Pilot Today** (Sep 25, 2021) — passed checkride on Cessna 150 at BMG Aviation
2. **A Beauty Came to KBMG** (Aug 30, 2021) — saw a Van's RV-7 at the airport
3. **Mark's RV7** (Aug 30, 2021) — visited Mark's workshop to see his RV-7 build in progress

How many hours for each?

Table now shows:

```
Workshop              4
Research & Education  31
Total                 35
──────────────────────
Flying & Social       5.17
```

Flying & Social is visually separated below the total with a divider line and doesn't affect the build total.
Two separate sidebar sections now — **Build Hours** with the total, and **Other Hours** below it with its own green header. Flying & Social sits cleanly outside the build count.

Right now it uses a hardcoded set `NON_BUILD_CATEGORIES = {"social-flying"}` — so any new category defaults to Build Hours unless you add it to that set in the script.

That's a friction point. Better approach: let the post itself declare which bucket it belongs to with a second attribute:

```html
<meta name="build-hours" content="2" data-category="social-flying" data-bucket="other">
```

Default (no `data-bucket`) = build hours. `data-bucket="other"` = Other Hours. The script reads it and routes accordingly — no need to ever touch the script for new categories.

Want me to update the script and the existing `social-flying` posts to use this?

Working. Now for any future category, the rule is simple — just set `data-bucket` in the post meta tag:

- `data-bucket="build"` (or omit it) → goes into Build Hours + Total
- `data-bucket="other"` → goes into Beyond the Build, excluded from Total

No script changes ever needed for new categories.

All done. Summary: - **89 images** downloaded across 11 posts into their respective `img/YEAR/post-slug/` folders - All placeholder divs replaced with real `` tags with captions where available - 0 broken image paths, 0 remaining placeholders - The only images not yet replaced are the ones on `index.html` and `about.html` which already have their real photos from earlier
Done. Profile photo downloaded to `img/common/` and wired up in `about.html`, capped at 260px wide so it doesn't dominate the layout.

Here are the posts tagged "Uncategorized" and what they should be:

| Post | Current | Suggested |
|---|---|---|
| Attended Fiberglass Workshop | Uncategorized | Research & Education |
| Back to Building! | Uncategorized | Planning |
| Mark's RV7 | Uncategorized | Flying & Social |

Does that look right to you?
No "Uncategorized" left in the build log. All three posts now have proper categories.
Fixed. No more "Uncategorized" on the homepage.

It's stale — copied from WordPress. The actual counts and categories have changed. Two options:

1. **Remove it** — the Build Log page already organizes by year, and categories are shown on each post. It's redundant.
2. **Update it** to reflect the real categories and counts from the actual posts.

The real breakdown across all 13 posts is:
- Empennage: 3
- Flying & Social: 3
- Planning: 5
- Research & Education: 2

Which do you prefer — remove it or update it?

Two options:

1. **Remove the links, just show counts** — simple, no dead links, honest about what the site is.
2. **Add anchor sections to `log.html` per category** — filter the log by category so clicking "Empennage (3)" jumps to a section listing only empennage posts.

Option 2 is more useful for readers and not much work — `log.html` already groups by year, we just add category anchors. But it means reorganizing `log.html` from year-grouped to category-grouped, or having both.

My recommendation: keep `log.html` year-grouped (that's the builder-site tradition), and make the category links simply filter by scrolling to an anchor that has all posts of that category listed. Essentially a second view at the bottom of `log.html`.

Or simplest: just remove the links and show plain text counts. Clean, no dead links, no extra pages needed.

What do you prefer?

Here's how `log.html` would look with both views:

---

**Build Log** `[ By Year | By Category ]`

---

**By Year**

**2026**
- Mar 25, 2026 — Attended Fiberglass Workshop `Research & Education`
- Feb 19, 2026 — Back to Building! `Planning`

**2022**
- Mar 19, 2022 — Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 2 `Empennage` — 49.3 hrs

**2021**
- Dec 1, 2021 — Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1 `Empennage` — 14.6 hrs
- ... (rest of 2021 posts)

---

**By Category**

**Empennage** (3)
- Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 2
- Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1
- Empennage Inventoried

**Planning** (5)
- Back to Building!
- Finished the Practice Flap Kit
- ...

**Research & Education** (2)
- Attended Fiberglass Workshop
- HomebuiltHelp RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD

**Flying & Social** (3)
- Became a Pilot Today
- A Beauty Came to KBMG
- Mark's RV7

---

The two sections sit on the same page, separated by a divider. The category sidebar links jump to the `#by-category` anchor. Simple, no extra pages, works well on mobile too.

Want me to build it?

Done. The log page now has:

- **By Year** section at the top (chronological, as before)
- **By Category** section below a divider, with anchors for each category
- Sidebar has "Jump To" links for both views and "Categories" links that jump directly to each category section
- `index.html` categories sidebar updated to match the new anchors and correct category names

Working perfectly. Now `python3 update-hours.py` does everything in one shot:

1. Scans all posts for `build-hours` → updates the hours tables in `index.html`
2. Scans all posts for `build-category` + `build-date` → regenerates the By Category section in `log.html` and the Categories sidebar in both `log.html` and `index.html`

Add a new post with the two meta tags and run the script — everything updates automatically.
Good call. How about `rebuild-site.py`? It captures that it regenerates derived content across the site from post metadata.
Done. The script is now `rebuild-site.py` and the README/TASKS reflect it.
Done. Year and category headings in the log now have a slightly larger size, green color, and a red left border — visually distinct from the post links below them.
Links sidebar now only on `index.html`.
Done.

That's a good design question. Two philosophies:

**Keep it as "posts around that time"** — each post's sidebar shows the 5 most recent posts *at the time it was written*. Gives a sense of context, like a newspaper archive. But it's static and never needs updating since it reflects that moment in time. This is actually how old builder sites worked.

**Keep it as "latest 5 posts" site-wide** — always shows the newest content, helps readers navigate to recent activity. But then it needs updating every time you add a post (another thing `rebuild-site.py` would have to manage across all post files).

My recommendation: **remove it from individual posts entirely** and instead rely on the Build Log page for navigation. The prev/next links at the bottom of each post already let readers browse chronologically. The sidebar on posts is getting crowded anyway — less is more.

Alternatively, keep just the prev/next nav (which you already have) and drop the Recent Posts sidebar from posts.

What do you think?

Good choice on keeping the contextual sidebar. For prev/next, here are some ideas:

**Option A — Plain arrows (current)**
```
← Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1        Back to Building! →
```

**Option B — Labeled with context**
```
‹ OLDER                                          NEWER ›
Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1        Back to Building!
```

**Option C — Newspaper style**
```
« Previously                              Up Next »
Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1        Back to Building!
```

**Option D — Date shown**
```
← Dec 1, 2021                              Feb 19, 2026 →
Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1        Back to Building!
```

**Option E — Full card style** (slightly more visual)
```
┌─────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐
│ ← Previous entry    │    │   Next entry →       │
│ Horiz. Stab Upd. 1  │    │   Back to Building!  │
│ Dec 1, 2021         │    │   Feb 19, 2026        │
└─────────────────────┘    └─────────────────────┘
```

I'd lean toward **Option D** — the date adds real value on a build log since the time gaps between posts tell a story (e.g. a 4-year gap between posts is meaningful). Option E is the most polished but adds more HTML per post.

Which direction appeals to you?

Good question. Right now prev/next is hardcoded in each post's HTML — so yes, every new post requires manually editing the previous post to add a "next" link. That's fragile. The clean solution: **let `rebuild-site.py` own prev/next**. Since it already reads all posts and knows their dates and paths, it can sort them chronologically and inject the prev/next nav into every post automatically. The workflow becomes: 1. Write new post HTML — **don't add prev/next links at all**, just leave a marker: ```html
``` 2. Run `python3 rebuild-site.py` 3. Script sorts all posts by date, finds each post's neighbors, and rewrites the `post-nav` div with the correct Option D style links and dates That's it. You never touch prev/next manually again. The only thing you need in each new post is the `build-date` meta tag (which you already need for the log anyway) — the script derives everything else from it. Want me to implement this?

Working perfectly. The nav for "Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 2" correctly shows:

```
← Dec 1, 2021                    Feb 19, 2026 →
  Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1    Back to Building!
```

From now on, adding a new post just requires the `build-date` meta tag — run `rebuild-site.py` and all 13 (soon more) posts get their prev/next updated automatically.

Same problem — it's hardcoded in every post sidebar. Let's have `rebuild-site.py` own it too. Add a marker and auto-generate the 5 most recent posts.

First, replace all hardcoded Recent Posts sections with a marker:

input.__tool_use_purpose: Replace hardcoded Recent Posts sections with marker in all posts
input.command:
cd /home/bchandio/work/workspace/uran-khatola-factory/website && python3 -c " import os, re pattern = re.compile(r'
\s*

Recent Posts

.*?
', re.DOTALL) marker = '
' for root, dirs, files in os.walk('posts'): for f in files: if not f.endswith('.html'): continue path = os.path.join(root, f) c = open(path).read() new = pattern.sub(marker, c) if new != c: open(path, 'w').write(new) print(f'Cleared: {path}') "

content.data:
You're right — "Recent Posts" in the sidebar of a post should show the 5 posts closest in time to that post, not the 5 newest site-wide. So it gives the reader context of what was happening around that period of the build.

That's still something `rebuild-site.py` can own — for each post, find the 2 before and 2 after it chronologically (plus itself = 5), and inject that as the sidebar. Want me to do that?

Yes exactly — and that's fine because `rebuild-site.py` already rewrites all posts in one shot anyway (it already updates post-nav in all 13 posts every run). Adding Recent Posts to that same loop costs nothing extra. You just run the script once after adding a post and everything across all posts updates.

Want me to proceed?

Because `update_post_nav` loops over every post in the `posts/` directory and rewrites the `post-nav` div in each one. It doesn't check if anything changed — it just rewrites all of them every time.

For post-nav that's necessary because adding post #14 changes post #13's "next" link — so #13 must be rewritten. But it also rewrites posts #1 through #12 even though nothing changed for them. It's slightly wasteful but harmless since it's just file writes on your local machine and takes milliseconds.

For Recent Posts (sliding window), same thing — adding post #14 shifts the window for posts #11, #12, #13. Posts further back are unaffected but the script would rewrite them anyway. Again harmless.

The alternative would be to only rewrite posts whose neighbors actually changed, but that's added complexity for no real benefit on a 13-post static site. Even at 500 posts it would still run in under a second.

Want me to proceed with the sliding window Recent Posts?

Working. The "Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 2" sidebar shows 2 newer posts above it, itself marked with «, and 2 older posts below. Fully automatic from now on.

On the deployment question — we'll tackle that separately. The short answer is `rsync` with `--checksum` so only changed files get uploaded, not the entire site.

All three files updated. Key things captured:

- **README.md** — full workflow for adding a post, media import steps, design tokens, deployment note
- **TASKS.md** — everything completed checked off, backlog organized into: deployment, content, design/UX, technical (including the rsync optimization discussion)
- **AGENT_CONTEXT.md** — complete post inventory table, all HTML conventions, what `rebuild-site.py` does, path depth rules, hours categories, current hours state
Exactly one "Beyond the Build" section now, and running the script again won't duplicate it.
Done. Sidebar order is now: Build Hours → Beyond the Build → Aircraft → Build Phases → Categories → Links.
Done. The drawing is already `display: block` from the global CSS rule, so `margin: 0 auto` centers it.

A few options:

**1. Browser DevTools (easiest, no extra tools)**  
Open the site in Chrome or Firefox, press `F12`, then click the phone/tablet icon (Toggle Device Toolbar). You can pick iPhone, Pixel, iPad etc. and see exactly how it renders.

**2. Firefox Responsive Design Mode**  
`F12` → click the phone icon, or `Ctrl+Shift+M`. Lets you drag to resize and pick device presets.

**3. Real device on same network**  
Start the local server:
```bash
cd website && python3 -m http.server 8080
```
Find your machine's local IP (`ip addr` or `hostname -I`), then open `http://192.168.x.x:8080` on your phone while on the same WiFi.

Option 3 is the most accurate since it's a real device with a real browser. Option 1/2 is faster for quick checks during development.

Which do you want to try?

Run this in your terminal:

```bash
cd /home/bchandio/work/workspace/uran-khatola-factory/website
python3 -m http.server 8080
```

Then on your phone (connected to the same WiFi), open:

```
http://192.168.1.67:8080
```

Keep the terminal running while you browse. `Ctrl+C` to stop when done.
fix my tmux conf file so that I can scroll using mouse and also have a larger history

tool.tool_use_purpose: Find tmux config file

content.data:
set -g mouse on
set -g history-limit 10000

tool.tool_use_purpose: Read existing tmux config

content.data: Mouse is already on and history is set to 10000. I'd bump the history limit — 10000 lines is modest. A common choice is 50000 or 100000.

input.__tool_use_purpose: Update history limit in tmux config
input.oldStr: set -g history-limit 10000
input.newStr: set -g history-limit 50000

content.data: Successfully replaced 1 occurrence(s) in /home/bchandio/.tmux.conf.

tool.tool_use_purpose: Update history limit in tmux config

FileWrite.oldStr: set -g history-limit 10000
FileWrite.newStr: set -g history-limit 50000

content.data: Your config already had `mouse on`, so scrolling was already enabled. I bumped `history-limit` from 10000 to 50000 lines. Run `tmux source ~/.tmux.conf` in an existing session to apply it without restarting.
I tried and scrolling doesn't work yet. Do I have to init it or something?

Did you run `tmux source ~/.tmux.conf` inside a tmux session? That's required to reload the config for existing sessions.

If you did and it still doesn't work, the issue is likely your terminal emulator intercepting scroll events before tmux sees them. Which terminal are you using?

Also, to scroll with mouse in tmux you need to click inside a pane first to focus it, then scroll. If you're in copy mode already, scroll should work. You can also enter copy mode manually with `prefix + [` then use the mouse wheel.
tmux source ~/.tmux.conf
Run that command inside a tmux session (not outside of one). If you're already in tmux, just paste it in the terminal and hit enter. After that, mouse scroll should work immediately.
do it

input.__tool_use_purpose: Reload tmux config

tool.tool_use_purpose: Reload tmux config

content.data: Done. Mouse scrolling should work now.
yes it works now

ok now we need to design and build a website. It is for my project that I am personally working on. I am building a Vans RV8 homebuilt aircraft in my garage. The website will provide log of my work progress and my thoughts as I continue working on it. Most people in the 90s and 2000s used to have thier websites of their build project. But now a days mostly people post on instagram and youtube, which I will also do but I also want to have a website as well. I already have a wordpress site that is blog oriented. I want more vintage fell in the website like basic HTML looking sites from the 90s and 2000s. Although we will put sophistication and new tech where it makes sense but the UI will be vintage. This is my wordpress site: https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/ that we will use to get started in term of the content. Let me also provide you some websites of other builders as an inspiration: https://www.aerovintage.com/rv8/rv8-index.htm https://www.rvplane.com/ http://smittysrv.com/search.asp https://tasrv14.blogspot.com/p/home.html http://papalimabravo.com/ http://rv8project.com/
Let me first look at your WordPress site and the inspiration sites to understand the content and aesthetic before designing anything.

# Home

I am building a small plane called Vans RV8. The goal is to build the plane, perform flight testing and maintenance and then eventually be able to perform longer range flights. The domain of work is in geography, manufacturing and assembly, aviation and gross root spread of the ideas of workmanship at home and local community level.

Vans RV8 (Drawing from Vans)

While living here in the United States of America, I have gotten this little Americanism of [building aircraft at home][9] ([read more here][10]). Exploration (and thereby aviation) has been very American to its core, and therefore, since the early days of aviation people in America have been building their own flying machines, experimenting and adventuring. [Barnstorming ][11]in the early 20th century was very much gross root and placed the common man at the center of what he can do and achieve, distinct from our modern experiences of regulations and “luxury” when we think and conceive of personal aviation. Living in America has given me some sense and value of the individual, his dreams and his freedom.

A completed and flying RV. Picture by Vans Aircraft RV8 Page

# Home
* [banner_tom_gray_RV8 (2)]

 

Welcome to Tom’s RV-8 Project website.  This is a chronical of a Van’s RV-8 kit airplane construction project which I plan to begin in December, 2015.  Once the process has started, this site will be updated weekly until it is completed.

In the meantime, be sure to visit the [RV-8 Info][11] section to learn more about this amazing high performance, two place tandem seating sport aerobatic airplane.  I have also created a [Shop Gallery ][12]section which contains various photo’s of my shop.

Enjoy and check back often to view the progress!

Disclaimer:  The information contained within this website is not intended to be used or referred to for instructional purposes so standard disclaimers apply.  Any construction methods posted within are shared for informational purposes only.  Please refer to [Van’s Aircraft][13] for building tips and/or instructional information.
© Copyright 2014 Toms RV-8 Project.  All Rights Reserved.

## RV-8 | *Fastback*


Welcome! This site chronicles the building, flying, and maintenance of my [Van’s Aircraft][29] [RV-8][30]—a two-seat, tailwheel, fully aerobatic, cross-country-capable aircraft designed to deliver “Total Performance.” I built the RV-8 from a kit over the course of 2,000 hours, and this blog is my way of sharing the journey with friends, family, and fellow builders. Building an airplane is a massive undertaking, and this is my way of paying it forward.

Along the way, I’ve expanded my ratings to include a [tailwheel endorsement][31], [CFI][32], [CFI-I][33], [Commercial Multi-Engine Add-on][34], and [MEI][35]. I also had the chance to pursue rotorcraft training—earning my [Private][36], [Instrument][37], and Commercial add-ons in helicopters.

If you’re interested in the technical side, I’m documenting all the work involved in the Show Planes Fastback build on a separate page—[check it out here][38].

You’ll also find index pages organized by topic. All my flight activities, including helicopter training, are available [here][39].

**LATEST POST SUMMARY:**

[Two Year Anniversary!][40] (5/22/2025) [143 Hours] - I haven’t barely posted since the first 6 months flying this airplane. But she has nearly 200 hours on her now and has flown all the way across the country. I think it is un-realistic for me to think that I will maintain every maintenance issue or flight on this site. I will however endeavor to […]
[Sunrise Over Manhattan: Flying the Hudson River Corridor][41] (11/29/2024) [2 Hours] - Flying the Hudson River Corridor at sunrise delivered jaw-dropping views of Manhattan—and a top-tier RV-8 memory.
[Cross Country by RV-8: A Farewell Flight Before Foreign Service][42] (7/1/2024) [40.3 Hours] - A 21-day cross-country adventure in the RV-8, from Maryland to Zion and back, filled with friends, flight challenges, and unforgettable views.
[Oil Change and Misc Maintenance][43] (7/19/2023) [3 Hours] -
[Nose Oil Leak, Part Three: The Prop, the Paper Towels, and the Real Culprit][44] (7/19/2023) [10 Hours] - After months chasing a phantom prop hub leak, the real culprit turned out to be a loose NPT fitting with the wrong sealant.
[Low Oil Pressure][45] (7/19/2023) [1 Hours] - The oil pressure has been on the low side of good since the first flight. Time to adjust the pressure relief valve.
[Nose Seal: Part deux][46] (7/3/2023) [3 Hours] - Investigating the nose seal oil leak.
[Induction snorkel & oil cooler bypass][47] (7/3/2023) [3 Hours] - Fiberglass relief to prevent cowl rubbing, and removing the leaky oil cooler bypass valve.
[Maintenance and Tuning – First report][48] (7/2/2023) [30 Hours] - Maintenance and tweaks in the first 7 hours of flying.
[Flying & Performance – First report][49] (7/2/2023) [6.3 Hours] - Taking a look at how the engine and airplane are performing with data analysis. Plus a few in-flight photos.
[First Flight!!!!][50] (6/15/2023) [1 Hours] - First flight of N22UP!!!!!
[Airworthiness Inspection!][51] (5/25/2023) - Inspection by my DAR for special airworthiness certificate - ISSUED!
[Final Airworthiness Inspection Prep][52] (5/24/2023) [8 Hours] - Inspecting everything one more time. Logbooks. Shop prep. Misc wrap up work.
[FAA Updates to Phase 1 Testing – Task Based Testing Approved][53] (5/18/2023) - New guidance allowing task-based testing during Phase 1.
[Airworthiness Application][54] (5/18/2023) - Paperwork for the airworthiness application.
[Pitot Static & Transponder Certification][55] (5/12/2023) [2 Hours] - Pitot static and transponder certification.
[Weight and Balance][56] (5/10/2023) [5 Hours] - Final weight and balance for N22UP prior to Airworthiness Application!
[N-Number, Inspection Panels, & Misc][57] (5/10/2023) [4 Hours] - Installing the N-Number, inspection panels, and other misc wrap up work.
[Dynamic Prop Balance][58] (5/10/2023) [3 Hours] - Using a DynaVibe classic to dynamically balance my propeller.
[Magneto Washers & Weight and Balance Prep][59] (5/4/2023) [2 Hours] - Installing new washers for the hold down on the magneto and installing covers for weight and balance.

 

[View Blog Format][60]

Maintenance: 51 hours

## RV-8 | *Fastback*


Welcome! This site chronicles the building, flying, and maintenance of my [Van’s Aircraft][29] [RV-8][30]—a two-seat, tailwheel, fully aerobatic, cross-country-capable aircraft designed to deliver “Total Performance.” I built the RV-8 from a kit over the course of 2,000 hours, and this blog is my way of sharing the journey with friends, family, and fellow builders. Building an airplane is a massive undertaking, and this is my way of paying it forward.

Along the way, I’ve expanded my ratings to include a [tailwheel endorsement][31], [CFI][32], [CFI-I][33], [Commercial Multi-Engine Add-on][34], and [MEI][35]. I also had the chance to pursue rotorcraft training—earning my [Private][36], [Instrument][37], and Commercial add-ons in helicopters.

If you’re interested in the technical side, I’m documenting all the work involved in the Show Planes Fastback build on a separate page—[check it out here][38].

You’ll also find index pages organized by topic. All my flight activities, including helicopter training, are available [here][39].

**LATEST POST SUMMARY:**

[Two Year Anniversary!][40] (5/22/2025) [143 Hours] - I haven’t barely posted since the first 6 months flying this airplane. But she has nearly 200 hours on her now and has flown all the way across the country. I think it is un-realistic for me to think that I will maintain every maintenance issue or flight on this site. I will however endeavor to […]
[Sunrise Over Manhattan: Flying the Hudson River Corridor][41] (11/29/2024) [2 Hours] - Flying the Hudson River Corridor at sunrise delivered jaw-dropping views of Manhattan—and a top-tier RV-8 memory.
[Cross Country by RV-8: A Farewell Flight Before Foreign Service][42] (7/1/2024) [40.3 Hours] - A 21-day cross-country adventure in the RV-8, from Maryland to Zion and back, filled with friends, flight challenges, and unforgettable views.
[Oil Change and Misc Maintenance][43] (7/19/2023) [3 Hours] -
[Nose Oil Leak, Part Three: The Prop, the Paper Towels, and the Real Culprit][44] (7/19/2023) [10 Hours] - After months chasing a phantom prop hub leak, the real culprit turned out to be a loose NPT fitting with the wrong sealant.
[Low Oil Pressure][45] (7/19/2023) [1 Hours] - The oil pressure has been on the low side of good since the first flight. Time to adjust the pressure relief valve.
[Nose Seal: Part deux][46] (7/3/2023) [3 Hours] - Investigating the nose seal oil leak.
[Induction snorkel & oil cooler bypass][47] (7/3/2023) [3 Hours] - Fiberglass relief to prevent cowl rubbing, and removing the leaky oil cooler bypass valve.
[Maintenance and Tuning – First report][48] (7/2/2023) [30 Hours] - Maintenance and tweaks in the first 7 hours of flying.
[Flying & Performance – First report][49] (7/2/2023) [6.3 Hours] - Taking a look at how the engine and airplane are performing with data analysis. Plus a few in-flight photos.
[First Flight!!!!][50] (6/15/2023) [1 Hours] - First flight of N22UP!!!!!
[Airworthiness Inspection!][51] (5/25/2023) - Inspection by my DAR for special airworthiness certificate - ISSUED!
[Final Airworthiness Inspection Prep][52] (5/24/2023) [8 Hours] - Inspecting everything one more time. Logbooks. Shop prep. Misc wrap up work.
[FAA Updates to Phase 1 Testing – Task Based Testing Approved][53] (5/18/2023) - New guidance allowing task-based testing during Phase 1.
[Airworthiness Application][54] (5/18/2023) - Paperwork for the airworthiness application.
[Pitot Static & Transponder Certification][55] (5/12/2023) [2 Hours] - Pitot static and transponder certification.
[Weight and Balance][56] (5/10/2023) [5 Hours] - Final weight and balance for N22UP prior to Airworthiness Application!
[N-Number, Inspection Panels, & Misc][57] (5/10/2023) [4 Hours] - Installing the N-Number, inspection panels, and other misc wrap up work.
[Dynamic Prop Balance][58] (5/10/2023) [3 Hours] - Using a DynaVibe classic to dynamically balance my propeller.
[Magneto Washers & Weight and Balance Prep][59] (5/4/2023) [2 Hours] - Installing new washers for the hold down on the magneto and installing covers for weight and balance.

 

[View Blog Format][60]

 

#### **Build: 2027.5 hours
#### **

** Flying: 192.6 hours
Maintenance: 51 hours

# Home
* [banner_tom_gray_RV8 (2)]

 

Welcome to Tom’s RV-8 Project website.  This is a chronical of a Van’s RV-8 kit airplane construction project which I plan to begin in December, 2015.  Once the process has started, this site will be updated weekly until it is completed.

In the meantime, be sure to visit the [RV-8 Info][11] section to learn more about this amazing high performance, two place tandem seating sport aerobatic airplane.  I have also created a [Shop Gallery ][12]section which contains various photo’s of my shop.

Enjoy and check back often to view the progress!

Disclaimer:  The information contained within this website is not intended to be used or referred to for instructional purposes so standard disclaimers apply.  Any construction methods posted within are shared for informational purposes only.  Please refer to [Van’s Aircraft][13] for building tips and/or instructional information.
© Copyright 2014 Toms RV-8 Project.  All Rights Reserved.

content.data:
Empty project. Let me plan this out before building.

Here's what I'm thinking based on what I've seen:

**Content (from your WordPress):**
- Home with intro about the RV-8 build
- Build Log (chronological entries like your blog posts)
- About
- Hours tracker (Workshop, Research, Empennage etc.)
- Links to YouTube/Facebook

**Structure inspired by the builder sites:**
- `papalimabravo.com` has the best organization: construction broken down by phase (Empennage, Wings, Fuselage, etc.)
- `rv8project.com` has the clean simple nav
- `rvplane.com` shows build hours prominently

**Vintage aesthetic I'm planning:**
- Fixed-width centered layout (~800px), like old sites
- System fonts or a monospace/serif combo
- Muted background (off-white or light gray), dark text
- Simple horizontal nav bar with borders
- Table-based-looking layout (but actual CSS, not tables)
- No animations, no gradients, no shadows — flat and honest
- A "Last Updated" line in the header
- Counter-style hours display

**Tech:**
- Pure HTML + CSS, no framework, no JS build step
- One CSS file
- Static site — easy to host anywhere (GitHub Pages, Netlify, etc.)
- Blog entries as static HTML pages (we can add a simple static site generator later if needed)

Does this match your vision? A few questions before I build:

1. Do you have a photo of your plane/workshop I can use as a placeholder, or should I use a text placeholder?
2. What's the site title — "Uran Khatola Factory" or something else?
3. Do you want the build log entries from WordPress ported in (the two posts: "Attended Fiberglass Workshop" and "Back to Building!")?
4. Any color preference? The classic builder sites use blues, greens, or just plain black/white. I'm leaning toward a cream/off-white background with dark navy text — very 2001 era.

1) use a placeholder for now. We will have lots of pictures as we add posts (pages) but for now lets settle on design, style, and the general scope of the website. 2) That is correct: Uran Khatola Factory. We can then have some sub title like building vans RV8 or building a plane in garage at home or soemthing?? 3) Please import all blog entries from my wordpress site and timestamp them according to their original time and date. 4) lets start of with your choice of colors but remember to not be too annoying to the eyes with bright colors. Also use a little bit of news UI theory on what users like but keep it vintage. Also think about how people using ipad and iphone/andriod will view it?
Let me fetch all the blog posts from your WordPress site first.

content.data:
[Skip to content][1]

[Uran Khatola Factory][2]

Building Flying Machines

☰ Menu
* [Home][3]
* [Blog][4]
* [About][5]
* [Education][6]
* [YouTube Channel][7]
* [Facebook Page][8]

# Blog

## [Attended Fiberglass Workshop][9]

[March 25, 2026][10] [bibrak][11][Leave a comment][12]

A few weeks ago I attended the EAA SportAir Fiberglass Techniques for RV Aircraft held at Fremont California. I can say that much of my anxiety around fiberglass, especially the canopy bonding and wheel pants has been relieved.

Posted in [Uncategorized][13]Tagged [aircraft][14], [EAA][15], [fiberglass][16], [homebuilt][17], [planing][18], [rv8][19], [vans][20], [Workshop][21]

## [Back to Building!][22]

[February 19, 2026][23] [bibrak][24][Leave a comment][25]

After about four years, I am back at building a plane. Life happened and I had to move four times, which impacted my progress on the RV-7 aircraft. I sold that empennage kit and my tools. I have since moved to Cupertino, California. This time, I am going to start building a Van’s RV-8. I thought if I were going to build a plane, it had better be a tandem. The RV-8 has a tandem seating arrangement where the pilot and the passenger/second pilot sit one behind the other, in a single-file line along the aircraft’s centerline, rather than side by side.

Vans RV8 (Picture from Van’s [RV8 website page][26])

The Van’s RV-8 was designed by Richard VanGrunsven. It is fully aerobatic-capable, able to perform loops, rolls, and other maneuvers. It is offered as a kit. You get thousands of small parts, blue print drawings, and build/assembly instructions.

[img_6929]Some empennage parts as I inventory them.

I have started inventorying the empennage kit parts and setting up my workshop, which for now is mostly in the living room. The empennage parts are not that large, so the living room should suffice until I move on to the next section, which will be either the fuselage or the wings.

Posted in [Uncategorized][27]Tagged [EAA][28], [planing][29], [rv8][30], [Workshop][31]

## [Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 2][32]

[March 19, 2022May 15, 2022][33] [bibrak][34][Leave a comment][35]

Time: 49.3 hours

Horizontal Stabilizer complete

Included work from 4th December 2021 to 17th March 2022.

I finished my first major component: the horizontal stabilizer. There were many unknowns and tricky situations that at times created setbacks and I had to reorder parts (that I ruined) or just had to sit back and think how to proceed.

A Ruined rib.

One of these tricky situation is the front spar assembly and the edge clearance issue, especially with HS-00005 ribs. Since the hole are not drilled on the flanges one has to align all the parts and then from there drill the holes. This often creates edge clearance issues if care is not taken. I was new and didn’t even know such a problem could exist. I ruined those ribs and had to order new ones. But since these hole are drill all along other parts, such as the doublers (HS-00001) and the angles (HS-710 and HS-714), it didn’t make much difference and I again ended up with edge clearance issues (not that severe though). So, I went ahead and fixed that with installing doubler on the HS-00005 ribs themselves and it provided the necessary edge distances.

The doubler to fix edge clearance.

Once that was taken care of, I deburred and prepared the parts. This involved the necessary dimpling on the skins and ribs and also priming. While dimpling one of the skins (HS-601PP) of the horizontal stabilizer I accidentally created a dimple hole. I asked Richard at our EAA 650 chapter and suggested tapping it close with a hammer and then applying JB Weld. At the the painter will fix any cosmetic irregularities.

Fixing the hole cause of dimpling mistake.

While, riveting I made the mistake of not using the right sized rivets in the front spar assembly. I later got myself a rivet gauge and took the guess work out (or in this case I should have actually read the plans drawings carefully, it is in the drawings). I had put AN470AD4-6 instead of -7. I drilled the wrong rivets out and installed the correct -7 size. Lessons learned: get your rivet gauge well before starting any rivets.

There was one other issue that I encountered with riveting the HS-707 rib to the skin. It had to do with me not fluting the leading edge of the rib. The issue was that one side of the rib was protruding out and was not properly sandwiched on to the skin. It was a big setback and I kept finding solutions. I eventually settled with using longer size rivet multiple times and failing. Eventually I enlarged to hole to #30 and put in the bigger diameter rivet and longer length. It helped but I wish I had the oops rivets. Now when I look back a better solution would have been to just take out the rivets on that side where it protruded and then be able to flute it. After that It would have settled properly and I would have easily riveted it. Lesson learned and I fluted the other side and there was no issue with the other side.

The leading edge of HS-707 protruding.

At the end when I riveted the skins and everything and it was the time to rivet the rear spar assembly I found that the read spar won’t align is any one side of the horizontal stabilizer. Oh the humanity! I sought help at the Facebook Van’s builder’s ground and there someone suggested perhaps fixing the 6 degree angle at the front spar and then it might align itself. I exactly did that the next day. The solution is to stretch the front assembly a little bit.

Rear spar misalignment.
Fixing misalignment by stretching.

The rear spar installation was quite simple and with that I completed the horizontal stabilizer. Now to the other parts: the vertical stabilizer.

The completed horizontal stabilizer.
Posted in [Empennage][36]Tagged [EAA][37], [Empennage][38], [homebuilt][39], [rv7][40]

## [Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1][41]

[December 1, 2021December 4, 2021][42] [bibrak][43][Leave a comment][44]

Time: 14.6 hours

Included work from October 2021 to 4th November 2021.

The plans call for starting with the horizontal stabilizer and in particular the rear spar assembly. At first everything was a bit overwhelming looking at different diagrams, physical parts and the instructions but my interest and the experience gained from practice kit helped me overcome.

I have made a decision to keep going with the work even if I have doubts and I mess up with parts. In that case, I will just order new parts. This will help me overcome fear and not waste much time. Also, I must add that the year 2021 and social media is a blessing. I have fellow builders connected on different social media platforms and blogs who share their experiences and wisdom. This has also been very helpful in clarifying doubts.

Anyways, so I started with the rear spar assembly, which was quite easy. Connected the spar reinforcement, smoothed them all out with bench belt sander and then with 3M wheel. The wheel in the drill press was much better than in hand with a high RPM air die grinder, which is great for smaller parts like ribs etc where I have the other 3M wheel already worn out and made into shape.

Initial smoothing of the spar reinforcement. Later I smoothed it more and primed it.
The 3M wheel and my mishandling cause those little dents. I tried to smooth them out as much as I could. Later I got a 400 grit sand paper and used that on the surface rather than using the 3M wheel. The 3M wheel in my experience is good for edges.
Rear spar assembly and doubly checking those unusual holes like the #21.
Front spar of the horizontal stabilizer is very tricky. There are many thing going on here so pay attention. The front spar of the horizontal stabilizer was tricky to understand first. There is too much going on and aligning everything with the tolerance is something to be careful about. Some log
39.169490 -86.536105
Posted in [Empennage][45]Tagged [EAA][46], [Empennage][47], [homebuilt][48], [rv7][49], [vans][50]

## [Empennage Inventoried][51]

[October 27, 2021October 27, 2021][52] [bibrak][53][Leave a comment][54]

It finally happened on the 25th of Oct 2021 that my kit arrived. I took it out to the hangar the next day with the help of Anil who has a bigger car so that the larger box fit in.

There were two items missing a small rib and the USB plans. I have asked vans for that. On the whole the kit was well packaged and arrived very well.

The inventorying took 3.5 hours. Now the real hard part starts and many thousand hours to go.

Posted in [Empennage][55]Tagged [Empennage][56]

## [Finished the Practice Flap Kit][57]

[October 20, 2021October 20, 2021][58] [bibrak][59][Leave a comment][60]

This flap practice kit is excellent in terms of providing the necessary skills and experience of most of the build process.

Completed practice flap

I went for this red oxide color primers just to figure out a primer scheme and color for the actual build. I think this is an over kill, especially for the parts that will be covered inside. I will from now on use a basic self etch primer by Rust-Oleum. So maybe later in areas that are open like the fuselage I might go for this or a white color.

The process. There were some mistakes made and I tried to fix them. After all it’s a practice project for a reason.
Posted in [Planning][61]Tagged [planing][62]

## [Became a Pilot Today. Sept 25th, 2021][63]

[September 25, 2021October 20, 2021][64] [bibrak][65][Leave a comment][66]

Passed my checkride today on the beloved N66591 Cessna 150 at BMG aviation Bloomingon IN.

Dave Ellis (on the left) helped me push towards it, very encouraging and helpful. Jim Jacobi was my examiner (black shirt on the right), very nice and cool as I answered almost all questioned in the oral without looking up.

Posted in [Flying & Planes][67]Tagged [flying][68]

## [A beauty Came to KBMG][69]

[August 30, 2021October 20, 2021][70] [bibrak][71][Leave a comment][72]

Someone at our airport KBMG bought this beautiful Vans RV7. It has a slider canopy and is conventional gear (tail wheel). I have been drooling over it recently.

Posted in [Flying & Planes][73]

## [Mark’s RV7][74]

[August 30, 2021August 30, 2021][75] [bibrak][76][Leave a comment][77]

I have finished setting up my workshop with tools and have started to learn how to use them. Along with doing that I am visiting other builders projects to get an idea of the scale of matters and how it actually looks like when the plane is about half done.

Mark opened the cowl to show me the details of the engine installation.

Mark who is an aeronautical engineer by profession was kind enough to show me his project at his home garage. He is building a Vans RV7 (same as I will) and is quite near completion. He started off with quick built fuselage and wing kit that saved him time and work. I am not sure whether I will go for the quick built. I will save time but I want to see the experience and knowledge of building those pieces. But we will see…

Mark showed me some places of work that were intricate and he made some mistakes. These gave me some ideas as I plane ahead. Specially with the cowling.

Looking at it all I could now appreciate the scale of work that is ahead of me.

Posted in [Uncategorized][78]

## [Getting to the know tools][79]

[July 11, 2021October 20, 2021][80] [bibrak][81][Leave a comment][82]

Fellow builders at our EAA Chapter 650 are very helpful. I have been spending time visiting hangers and workshops to get a feeling of various projects at different stages of construction. It has helped in getting to know what tools I must acquire for my build.

The tools such as a band saw, air compressor, drill press and a sander are a must at the workbench. But then there are all these pneumatic tools that I wouldn’t have known, least how to use and where to use, had it not been for the experienced builder showing me around. This would have been very overwhelming.

Richard showing me how to smooth out aluminum edges using air grinder. Aluminum must be smoothed out to avoid any cracks in the structure in the future.

Russ’s tools at his workshop

More tools
Before inserting you need oil.
Russ’s project and his tools. Guess the plane?
Posted in [Planning][83]Tagged [EAA][84], [tools][85], [Workshop][86]

[Skip to content][1]

[Uran Khatola Factory][2]

Building Flying Machines

☰ Menu
* [Home][3]
* [Blog][4]
* [About][5]

* [Facebook Page][8]

# Blog

## [Attended Fiberglass Workshop][9]

[March 25, 2026][10] [bibrak][11][Leave a comment][12]

A few weeks ago I attended the EAA SportAir Fiberglass Techniques for RV Aircraft held at Fremont California. I can say that much of my anxiety around fiberglass, especially the canopy bonding and wheel pants has been relieved.

Posted in [Uncategorized][13]Tagged [aircraft][14], [EAA][15], [fiberglass][16], [homebuilt][17], [planing][18], [rv8][19], [vans][20], [Workshop][21]

## [Back to Building!][22]

[February 19, 2026][23] [bibrak][24][Leave a comment][25]

After about four years, I am back at building a plane. Life happened and I had to move four times, which impacted my progress on the RV-7 aircraft. I sold that empennage kit and my tools. I have since moved to Cupertino, California. This time, I am going to start building a Van’s RV-8. I thought if I were going to build a plane, it had better be a tandem. The RV-8 has a tandem seating arrangement where the pilot and the passenger/second pilot sit one behind the other, in a single-file line along the aircraft’s centerline, rather than side by side.

Vans RV8 (Picture from Van’s [RV8 website page][26])

The Van’s RV-8 was designed by Richard VanGrunsven. It is fully aerobatic-capable, able to perform loops, rolls, and other maneuvers. It is offered as a kit. You get thousands of small parts, blue print drawings, and build/assembly instructions.

[img_6929]Some empennage parts as I inventory them.

I have started inventorying the empennage kit parts and setting up my workshop, which for now is mostly in the living room. The empennage parts are not that large, so the living room should suffice until I move on to the next section, which will be either the fuselage or the wings.

Posted in [Uncategorized][27]Tagged [EAA][28], [planing][29], [rv8][30], [Workshop][31]

## [Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 2][32]

[March 19, 2022May 15, 2022][33] [bibrak][34][Leave a comment][35]

Time: 49.3 hours

Horizontal Stabilizer complete

Included work from 4th December 2021 to 17th March 2022.

I finished my first major component: the horizontal stabilizer. There were many unknowns and tricky situations that at times created setbacks and I had to reorder parts (that I ruined) or just had to sit back and think how to proceed.

A Ruined rib.

One of these tricky situation is the front spar assembly and the edge clearance issue, especially with HS-00005 ribs. Since the hole are not drilled on the flanges one has to align all the parts and then from there drill the holes. This often creates edge clearance issues if care is not taken. I was new and didn’t even know such a problem could exist. I ruined those ribs and had to order new ones. But since these hole are drill all along other parts, such as the doublers (HS-00001) and the angles (HS-710 and HS-714), it didn’t make much difference and I again ended up with edge clearance issues (not that severe though). So, I went ahead and fixed that with installing doubler on the HS-00005 ribs themselves and it provided the necessary edge distances.

The doubler to fix edge clearance.

Once that was taken care of, I deburred and prepared the parts. This involved the necessary dimpling on the skins and ribs and also priming. While dimpling one of the skins (HS-601PP) of the horizontal stabilizer I accidentally created a dimple hole. I asked Richard at our EAA 650 chapter and suggested tapping it close with a hammer and then applying JB Weld. At the the painter will fix any cosmetic irregularities.

Fixing the hole cause of dimpling mistake.

While, riveting I made the mistake of not using the right sized rivets in the front spar assembly. I later got myself a rivet gauge and took the guess work out (or in this case I should have actually read the plans drawings carefully, it is in the drawings). I had put AN470AD4-6 instead of -7. I drilled the wrong rivets out and installed the correct -7 size. Lessons learned: get your rivet gauge well before starting any rivets.

There was one other issue that I encountered with riveting the HS-707 rib to the skin. It had to do with me not fluting the leading edge of the rib. The issue was that one side of the rib was protruding out and was not properly sandwiched on to the skin. It was a big setback and I kept finding solutions. I eventually settled with using longer size rivet multiple times and failing. Eventually I enlarged to hole to #30 and put in the bigger diameter rivet and longer length. It helped but I wish I had the oops rivets. Now when I look back a better solution would have been to just take out the rivets on that side where it protruded and then be able to flute it. After that It would have settled properly and I would have easily riveted it. Lesson learned and I fluted the other side and there was no issue with the other side.

The leading edge of HS-707 protruding.

At the end when I riveted the skins and everything and it was the time to rivet the rear spar assembly I found that the read spar won’t align is any one side of the horizontal stabilizer. Oh the humanity! I sought help at the Facebook Van’s builder’s ground and there someone suggested perhaps fixing the 6 degree angle at the front spar and then it might align itself. I exactly did that the next day. The solution is to stretch the front assembly a little bit.

Rear spar misalignment.
Fixing misalignment by stretching.

The rear spar installation was quite simple and with that I completed the horizontal stabilizer. Now to the other parts: the vertical stabilizer.

The completed horizontal stabilizer.
Posted in [Empennage][36]Tagged [EAA][37], [Empennage][38], [homebuilt][39], [rv7][40]

## [Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1][41]

[December 1, 2021December 4, 2021][42] [bibrak][43][Leave a comment][44]

Time: 14.6 hours

Included work from October 2021 to 4th November 2021.

The plans call for starting with the horizontal stabilizer and in particular the rear spar assembly. At first everything was a bit overwhelming looking at different diagrams, physical parts and the instructions but my interest and the experience gained from practice kit helped me overcome.

I have made a decision to keep going with the work even if I have doubts and I mess up with parts. In that case, I will just order new parts. This will help me overcome fear and not waste much time. Also, I must add that the year 2021 and social media is a blessing. I have fellow builders connected on different social media platforms and blogs who share their experiences and wisdom. This has also been very helpful in clarifying doubts.

Anyways, so I started with the rear spar assembly, which was quite easy. Connected the spar reinforcement, smoothed them all out with bench belt sander and then with 3M wheel. The wheel in the drill press was much better than in hand with a high RPM air die grinder, which is great for smaller parts like ribs etc where I have the other 3M wheel already worn out and made into shape.

Initial smoothing of the spar reinforcement. Later I smoothed it more and primed it.
The 3M wheel and my mishandling cause those little dents. I tried to smooth them out as much as I could. Later I got a 400 grit sand paper and used that on the surface rather than using the 3M wheel. The 3M wheel in my experience is good for edges.
Rear spar assembly and doubly checking those unusual holes like the #21.
Front spar of the horizontal stabilizer is very tricky. There are many thing going on here so pay attention. The front spar of the horizontal stabilizer was tricky to understand first. There is too much going on and aligning everything with the tolerance is something to be careful about. Some log
39.169490 -86.536105
Posted in [Empennage][45]Tagged [EAA][46], [Empennage][47], [homebuilt][48], [rv7][49], [vans][50]

## [Empennage Inventoried][51]

[October 27, 2021October 27, 2021][52] [bibrak][53][Leave a comment][54]

It finally happened on the 25th of Oct 2021 that my kit arrived. I took it out to the hangar the next day with the help of Anil who has a bigger car so that the larger box fit in.

There were two items missing a small rib and the USB plans. I have asked vans for that. On the whole the kit was well packaged and arrived very well.

The inventorying took 3.5 hours. Now the real hard part starts and many thousand hours to go.

Posted in [Empennage][55]Tagged [Empennage][56]

## [Finished the Practice Flap Kit][57]

[October 20, 2021October 20, 2021][58] [bibrak][59][Leave a comment][60]

This flap practice kit is excellent in terms of providing the necessary skills and experience of most of the build process.

Completed practice flap

I went for this red oxide color primers just to figure out a primer scheme and color for the actual build. I think this is an over kill, especially for the parts that will be covered inside. I will from now on use a basic self etch primer by Rust-Oleum. So maybe later in areas that are open like the fuselage I might go for this or a white color.

The process. There were some mistakes made and I tried to fix them. After all it’s a practice project for a reason.
Posted in [Planning][61]Tagged [planing][62]

## [Became a Pilot Today. Sept 25th, 2021][63]

[September 25, 2021October 20, 2021][64] [bibrak][65][Leave a comment][66]

Passed my checkride today on the beloved N66591 Cessna 150 at BMG aviation Bloomingon IN.

Dave Ellis (on the left) helped me push towards it, very encouraging and helpful. Jim Jacobi was my examiner (black shirt on the right), very nice and cool as I answered almost all questioned in the oral without looking up.

Posted in [Flying & Planes][67]Tagged [flying][68]

## [A beauty Came to KBMG][69]

[August 30, 2021October 20, 2021][70] [bibrak][71][Leave a comment][72]

Someone at our airport KBMG bought this beautiful Vans RV7. It has a slider canopy and is conventional gear (tail wheel). I have been drooling over it recently.

Posted in [Flying & Planes][73]

## [Mark’s RV7][74]

[August 30, 2021August 30, 2021][75] [bibrak][76][Leave a comment][77]

I have finished setting up my workshop with tools and have started to learn how to use them. Along with doing that I am visiting other builders projects to get an idea of the scale of matters and how it actually looks like when the plane is about half done.

Mark opened the cowl to show me the details of the engine installation.

Mark who is an aeronautical engineer by profession was kind enough to show me his project at his home garage. He is building a Vans RV7 (same as I will) and is quite near completion. He started off with quick built fuselage and wing kit that saved him time and work. I am not sure whether I will go for the quick built. I will save time but I want to see the experience and knowledge of building those pieces. But we will see…

Mark showed me some places of work that were intricate and he made some mistakes. These gave me some ideas as I plane ahead. Specially with the cowling.

Looking at it all I could now appreciate the scale of work that is ahead of me.

Posted in [Uncategorized][78]

## [Getting to the know tools][79]

[July 11, 2021October 20, 2021][80] [bibrak][81][Leave a comment][82]

Fellow builders at our EAA Chapter 650 are very helpful. I have been spending time visiting hangers and workshops to get a feeling of various projects at different stages of construction. It has helped in getting to know what tools I must acquire for my build.

The tools such as a band saw, air compressor, drill press and a sander are a must at the workbench. But then there are all these pneumatic tools that I wouldn’t have known, least how to use and where to use, had it not been for the experienced builder showing me around. This would have been very overwhelming.

Richard showing me how to smooth out aluminum edges using air grinder. Aluminum must be smoothed out to avoid any cracks in the structure in the future.

Russ’s tools at his workshop

More tools
Before inserting you need oil.
Russ’s project and his tools. Guess the plane?
Posted in [Planning][83]Tagged [EAA][84], [tools][85], [Workshop][86]

content.data:
[Skip to content][1]

[Uran Khatola Factory][2]

Building Flying Machines

☰ Menu
* [Home][3]
* [Blog][4]
* [About][5]
* [Education][6]
* [YouTube Channel][7]
* [Facebook Page][8]

# Blog

## [Update on Empennage Crating Date][9]

[May 12, 2021May 15, 2021][10] [bibrak][11][Leave a comment][12]
Crate Date Letter from Vans

Received a letter from Vans that I need to sign and send them. The purpose of the letter is two fold: first to confirm that my kit is in the queue and; second to have me look at the complete order and attest that everything looks OK.

There you can find the expected crating date of: September 2021. This would mean that I should be expecting it to be at my door steps maybe later September 2021.

Now, I will just patiently wait and use the time to better educate myself about the Blueprints (plans) that I have also ordered and should be coming anytime soon.

Posted in [Planning][13]Tagged [EAA][14], [rv7][15], [vans][16]

## [HomebuiltHelp RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD][17]

[May 11, 2021May 11, 2021][18] [bibrak][19][Leave a comment][20]
RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD by [HomebuiltHelp][21]

While my tail it arrives, I decided to spend my time educating myself about the basics of construction, tools needed, how to use these tools and most importantly the tail kit itself. For that purpose, I ordered the [RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD][22] and the Preview Plans for RV7 from Vans. When the Preview Plans arrive I will get a birds eye view of the entire project and also some specific details that might help me in my planing and using this time before the kit comes. As for now (and today) the DVD arrived and I managed to watch it.

The DVD started off by talking about basics tools needed, their usage, aluminum sheet metal types, rivets, riveting, back-riveting, dimpling and countersinking, rivet removal. These were not just talked about in technical detail but were demonstrated step by step.

After that, the actual RV7 Rudder was built and shown in steps. This really helped me grasp as to what was coming. I had this lingering sense of being lost in complexities and what is to come. The DVD demo of the rudder assembly has cleared most of it and now I can at least know what to except and where to begin.

**Education and Research: 1 hour**

Posted in [Planning][23]Tagged [EAA][24], [homebuilt][25], [rudder][26], [rv7][27], [Workshop][28]

## [Ordered the Empennage Kit (Tail)][29]

[April 26, 2021April 30, 2021][30] [bibrak][31][Leave a comment][32]

Today, I placed my order for RV7 tail kit. The option I choose was with the electric trim and not the manual. I don’t know but I thought that it will be just easy to wire an electric cable at the end than to work with cables and pulley for the manual trim.

I want to build the plane with less intricacies as I have some time constraints being in the USA. I also realize that I will not be compromising on the quality of the build as this is going to be the plane I will be flying across the continents to Pakistan.

*Email confirming the order and 25% payment required*

And so I paid the 25% on it, which was $620. Now the time to wait. And wait for about 4 months is what they said. These large wait times for supposedly due to large amount of people stuck at home due to COVID and building planes.

In that regard, I also must start thinking about ordering my wing or the fuselage kit soon since the wait times on those are about 6 months (plus shipping).

I also need to now form my little workshop, which is from now on called the “Auran Khatola Factory (AKF)”. I am looking to move into a house that has a nice garage as I will not only need the space but my air tool will make noise. I don’t want to disturb the neighbours as it is not just annoying but will delay my build. I need that peace of mind and so do my neighbours.

More about the Vans RV7: [https://www.vansaircraft.com/rv-7/][33]

*An RV7 landing in sunset. Photo from Vans Website*
Posted in [Planning][34]Tagged [orders][35], [planing][36]

# About

[profile picture]

Bibrak

bibrakc at g mail dot com
* [Facebook][9]
* [Twitter][10]
* [Instagram][11]
* [LinkedIn][12]
* [YouTube][13]

## Bibrak is building a small plane that he hopes to fly back to Pakistan

The plane that I am building is a Vans RV8: a small experimental plane. The meaning of this project is to learn how to build, test and maintain general aviation planes. The skills, connections and experiences gained from this endeavor will help to not only inspire people to achieve long term high risk goals but also will help Pakistan to adopt basic manufacturing skills.

input.content:
Uran Khatola Factory

Building a Van's RV-8 in the Garage — One Rivet at a Time
  • Home
  • Build Log
  • About
  • YouTube
  • Facebook

I am building a Van's RV-8 — a small, fully aerobatic, two-seat experimental aircraft — in my garage in Cupertino, California. The goal is to build it, fly it, and eventually take it on a long-range journey back to Pakistan. This site is a chronicle of that build: the work, the mistakes, the lessons, and the progress.

The RV-8 is a tandem-seat, tailwheel aircraft designed by Richard VanGrunsven. It is offered as a kit: thousands of parts, blueprint drawings, and assembly instructions. You build it yourself.

[Photo: Van's RV-8 in flight — placeholder]

Recent Build Log Entries

  • March 25, 2026 Attended Fiberglass Workshop Uncategorized
  • February 19, 2026 Back to Building! Uncategorized
  • March 19, 2022 Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 2 Empennage
  • December 1, 2021 Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1 Empennage
  • October 27, 2021 Empennage Inventoried Empennage

→ View full build log

About This Project

While living in the United States, I picked up what I call a little Americanism: building aircraft at home. Exploration and aviation have been deeply American from the start — from the barnstormers of the early 20th century to the thousands of homebuilders today registered with the EAA. The common man, his tools, his garage, and his dream of flight.

The domain of this project spans geography, manufacturing, aviation, and the grassroots spread of workmanship at the home and community level. Read more →

Build Hours

Workshop10
Research & Education15
Empennage63.9
Total88.9

Build Phases

  • — Wings
  • — Fuselage
  • — Firewall Forward
  • — Finishing

input.content:
Uran Khatola Factory

Building a Van's RV-8 in the Garage — One Rivet at a Time
  • Home
  • Build Log
  • About
  • YouTube
  • Facebook

About

[Photo: Bibrak — placeholder]

Bibrak Qamar Chandio

bibrakc [at] gmail [dot] com

Bibrak is building a small plane that he hopes to fly back to Pakistan

The plane I am building is a Van's RV-8: a small experimental aircraft. The meaning of this project is to learn how to build, test, and maintain general aviation aircraft. The skills, connections, and experiences gained from this endeavor will help not only to inspire people to achieve long-term, high-risk goals, but will also help Pakistan adopt basic manufacturing skills.

While living here in the United States of America, I have gotten this little Americanism of building aircraft at home. Exploration — and thereby aviation — has been very American to its core. Since the early days of aviation, people in America have been building their own flying machines, experimenting and adventuring. Barnstorming in the early 20th century was very much grassroots and placed the common man at the center of what he can do and achieve, distinct from our modern experiences of regulations and "luxury" when we think of personal aviation.

Living in America has given me some sense and value of the individual, his dreams, and his freedom.

The Aircraft

The Van's RV-8 was designed by Richard VanGrunsven. It is a two-seat, tandem-configuration, tailwheel aircraft that is fully aerobatic — capable of loops, rolls, and other maneuvers. It is offered as a kit: thousands of small parts, blueprint drawings, and build/assembly instructions. You build it yourself, in your garage, over several thousand hours.

The Workshop

The workshop is currently in my living room and garage in Cupertino, California. The empennage parts are small enough to work on indoors. As the build progresses to larger sections — wings, fuselage — I will need more space.

Attended Fiberglass Workshop

📅 March 25, 2026 📁 Uncategorized

A few weeks ago I attended the EAA SportAir Fiberglass Techniques for RV Aircraft held at Fremont, California. I can say that much of my anxiety around fiberglass — especially the canopy bonding and wheel pants — has been relieved.

[Photo: EAA SportAir Fiberglass Workshop — placeholder]

input.__tool_use_purpose: Create post: Back to Building
input.content:
Uran Khatola Factory

Building a Van's RV-8 in the Garage — One Rivet at a Time
  • Home
  • Build Log
  • About
  • YouTube
  • Facebook

Back to Building!

📅 February 19, 2026 📁 Uncategorized

After about four years, I am back at building a plane. Life happened and I had to move four times, which impacted my progress on the RV-7 aircraft. I sold that empennage kit and my tools. I have since moved to Cupertino, California.

This time, I am going to start building a Van's RV-8. I thought if I were going to build a plane, it had better be a tandem. The RV-8 has a tandem seating arrangement where the pilot and the passenger/second pilot sit one behind the other, in a single-file line along the aircraft's centerline, rather than side by side.

[Photo: Van's RV-8 — placeholder]

Van's RV-8 (Picture from Van's RV-8 website page)

The Van's RV-8 was designed by Richard VanGrunsven. It is fully aerobatic-capable, able to perform loops, rolls, and other maneuvers. It is offered as a kit. You get thousands of small parts, blueprint drawings, and build/assembly instructions.

[Photo: Empennage parts during inventory — placeholder]

Some empennage parts as I inventory them.

I have started inventorying the empennage kit parts and setting up my workshop, which for now is mostly in the living room. The empennage parts are not that large, so the living room should suffice until I move on to the next section, which will be either the fuselage or the wings.

Tags: EAA rv8 workshop
