input.content:
Uran Khatola Factory

Building a Van's RV-8 in the Garage — One Rivet at a Time
  • Home
  • Build Log
  • About
  • YouTube
  • Facebook

Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 2

📅 March 19, 2022 📁 Empennage ⏱ 49.3 hours

Included work from 4th December 2021 to 17th March 2022.

I finished my first major component: the horizontal stabilizer. There were many unknowns and tricky situations that at times created setbacks and I had to reorder parts (that I ruined) or just had to sit back and think how to proceed.

[Photo: Completed horizontal stabilizer — placeholder]

Horizontal Stabilizer complete.

Edge Clearance Issues

One of these tricky situations is the front spar assembly and the edge clearance issue, especially with HS-00005 ribs. Since the holes are not drilled on the flanges, one has to align all the parts and then drill the holes from there. This often creates edge clearance issues if care is not taken. I was new and didn't even know such a problem could exist. I ruined those ribs and had to order new ones. But since these holes are drilled all along other parts — such as the doublers (HS-00001) and the angles (HS-710 and HS-714) — it didn't make much difference and I again ended up with edge clearance issues (not that severe though). So I went ahead and fixed that by installing a doubler on the HS-00005 ribs themselves, which provided the necessary edge distances.

[Photo: Ruined rib — placeholder]

A ruined rib.

[Photo: Doubler to fix edge clearance — placeholder]

The doubler to fix edge clearance.

Dimpling Mistake

Once that was taken care of, I deburred and prepared the parts. This involved the necessary dimpling on the skins and ribs and also priming. While dimpling one of the skins (HS-601PP) I accidentally created a dimple hole. I asked Richard at our EAA 650 chapter and he suggested tapping it closed with a hammer and then applying JB Weld. The painter will fix any cosmetic irregularities.

[Photo: Fixing the dimpling hole — placeholder]

Fixing the hole caused by the dimpling mistake.

Wrong Rivet Size

While riveting I made the mistake of not using the right sized rivets in the front spar assembly. I later got myself a rivet gauge and took the guesswork out. I had put AN470AD4-6 instead of -7. I drilled the wrong rivets out and installed the correct -7 size. Lesson learned: get your rivet gauge well before starting any rivets.

HS-707 Rib Issue

There was one other issue with riveting the HS-707 rib to the skin. It had to do with me not fluting the leading edge of the rib. One side of the rib was protruding out and was not properly sandwiched onto the skin. I eventually enlarged the hole to #30 and put in a bigger diameter, longer rivet. It helped, but I wish I had oops rivets. Now when I look back, a better solution would have been to just take out the rivets on that side where it protruded, then flute it. After that it would have settled properly. I fluted the other side and there was no issue.

[Photo: Leading edge of HS-707 protruding — placeholder]

The leading edge of HS-707 protruding.

Rear Spar Misalignment

At the end, when I riveted the skins and it was time to rivet the rear spar assembly, I found that the rear spar wouldn't align on one side of the horizontal stabilizer. I sought help at the Facebook Van's builders group and someone suggested fixing the 6-degree angle at the front spar, which might cause it to align itself. I did exactly that the next day — the solution is to stretch the front assembly a little bit.

[Photo: Rear spar misalignment — placeholder]

Rear spar misalignment.

[Photo: Fixing misalignment by stretching — placeholder]

Fixing misalignment by stretching.

The rear spar installation was quite simple and with that I completed the horizontal stabilizer. Now on to the vertical stabilizer.

[Photo: Completed horizontal stabilizer — placeholder]

The completed horizontal stabilizer.

Back to Building!

📅 February 19, 2026 📁 Uncategorized

After about four years, I am back at building a plane. Life happened and I had to move four times, which impacted my progress on the RV-7 aircraft. I sold that empennage kit and my tools. I have since moved to Cupertino, California.

This time, I am going to start building a Van's RV-8. I thought if I were going to build a plane, it had better be a tandem. The RV-8 has a tandem seating arrangement where the pilot and the passenger/second pilot sit one behind the other, in a single-file line along the aircraft's centerline, rather than side by side.

[Photo: Van's RV-8 — placeholder]

Van's RV-8 (Picture from Van's RV-8 website page)

The Van's RV-8 was designed by Richard VanGrunsven. It is fully aerobatic-capable, able to perform loops, rolls, and other maneuvers. It is offered as a kit. You get thousands of small parts, blueprint drawings, and build/assembly instructions.

[Photo: Empennage parts during inventory — placeholder]

Some empennage parts as I inventory them.

I have started inventorying the empennage kit parts and setting up my workshop, which for now is mostly in the living room. The empennage parts are not that large, so the living room should suffice until I move on to the next section, which will be either the fuselage or the wings.

Uran Khatola Factory

Building a Van's RV-8 in the Garage — One Rivet at a Time
  • Home

Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1

📅 December 1, 2021 📁 Empennage ⏱ 14.6 hours

Included work from October 2021 to 4th November 2021.

The plans call for starting with the horizontal stabilizer and in particular the rear spar assembly. At first everything was a bit overwhelming looking at different diagrams, physical parts, and the instructions — but my interest and the experience gained from the practice kit helped me overcome.

I have made a decision to keep going with the work even if I have doubts and I mess up parts. In that case, I will just order new parts. This will help me overcome fear and not waste much time. Also, I must add that the year 2021 and social media is a blessing. I have fellow builders connected on different social media platforms and blogs who share their experiences and wisdom. This has also been very helpful in clarifying doubts.

Rear Spar Assembly

I started with the rear spar assembly, which was quite easy. Connected the spar reinforcement, smoothed them all out with a bench belt sander and then with a 3M wheel. The wheel in the drill press was much better than in hand with a high RPM air die grinder, which is great for smaller parts like ribs where I have the other 3M wheel already worn out and shaped.

[Photo: Initial smoothing of spar reinforcement — placeholder]

Initial smoothing of the spar reinforcement. Later I smoothed it more and primed it.

[Photo: 3M wheel dents — placeholder]

The 3M wheel and my mishandling caused those little dents. I tried to smooth them out. Later I used 400 grit sandpaper on the surface rather than the 3M wheel. The 3M wheel in my experience is good for edges.

[Photo: Rear spar assembly — placeholder]

Rear spar assembly and double-checking those unusual holes like the #21.

Front Spar

The front spar of the horizontal stabilizer was tricky to understand at first. There is too much going on and aligning everything within tolerance is something to be careful about. Pay attention here — there are many things happening simultaneously.

Empennage Inventoried

📅 October 27, 2021 📁 Empennage

It finally happened on the 25th of October 2021 that my kit arrived. I took it out to the hangar the next day with the help of Anil, who has a bigger car so that the larger box would fit.

[Photo: Empennage kit arrival — placeholder]

There were two items missing: a small rib and the USB plans. I have asked Van's for those. On the whole the kit was well packaged and arrived in very good condition.

The inventorying took 3.5 hours. Now the real hard part starts — and many thousand hours to go.

Finished the Practice Flap Kit

📅 October 20, 2021 📁 Planning

This flap practice kit is excellent in terms of providing the necessary skills and experience of most of the build process.

[Photo: Completed practice flap — placeholder]

Completed practice flap.

I went with a red oxide color primer just to figure out a primer scheme and color for the actual build. I think this is an overkill, especially for the parts that will be covered inside. I will from now on use a basic self-etch primer by Rust-Oleum. So maybe later in areas that are open, like the fuselage, I might go for this or a white color.

[Photo: Practice flap build process — placeholder]

The process. There were some mistakes made and I tried to fix them. After all, it's a practice project for a reason.

Finished the Practice Flap Kit

📅 October 20, 2021 📁 Planning

This flap practice kit is excellent in terms of providing the necessary skills and experience of most of the build process.

[Photo: Completed practice flap — placeholder]

Completed practice flap.

I went with a red oxide color primer just to figure out a primer scheme and color for the actual build. I think this is overkill, especially for parts that will be covered inside. I will from now on use a basic self-etch primer by Rust-Oleum. Maybe later in areas that are open, like the fuselage, I might go for this or a white color.

[Photo: Practice flap build process — placeholder]

The process. There were some mistakes made and I tried to fix them. After all, it's a practice project for a reason.

Mark's RV7

📅 August 30, 2021 📁 Uncategorized

I have finished setting up my workshop with tools and have started to learn how to use them. Along with doing that I am visiting other builders' projects to get an idea of the scale of matters and how it actually looks when the plane is about half done.

Mark, who is an aeronautical engineer by profession, was kind enough to show me his project at his home garage. He is building a Van's RV-7 and is quite near completion. He started off with a quick-built fuselage and wing kit that saved him time and work. I am not sure whether I will go for the quick-built. I will save time but I want the experience and knowledge of building those pieces. But we will see.

[Photo: Mark's RV7 with cowl open — placeholder]

Mark opened the cowl to show me the details of the engine installation.

Mark showed me some places of work that were intricate and where he made some mistakes. These gave me some ideas as I plan ahead — especially with the cowling.

Looking at it all, I could now appreciate the scale of work that is ahead of me.

← Getting to Know the Tools A Beauty Came to KBMG →

Getting to Know the Tools

📅 July 11, 2021 📁 Planning

Fellow builders at our EAA Chapter 650 are very helpful. I have been spending time visiting hangars and workshops to get a feeling of various projects at different stages of construction. It has helped in getting to know what tools I must acquire for my build.

Tools such as a band saw, air compressor, drill press, and a sander are a must at the workbench. But then there are all these pneumatic tools that I wouldn't have known — least how to use and where to use them — had it not been for experienced builders showing me around. This would have been very overwhelming otherwise.

[Photo: Richard showing aluminum edge smoothing — placeholder]

Richard showing me how to smooth out aluminum edges using an air grinder. Aluminum must be smoothed out to avoid any cracks in the structure in the future.

[Photo: Russ's tools at his workshop — placeholder]

Russ's tools at his workshop.

[Photo: More tools — placeholder]
Tags: EAA tools workshop

Update on Empennage Crating Date

📅 May 12, 2021 📁 Planning
[Photo: Crate date letter from Van's — placeholder]

Crate Date Letter from Van's.

Received a letter from Van's that I need to sign and send back. The purpose of the letter is twofold: first, to confirm that my kit is in the queue; and second, to have me look at the complete order and attest that everything looks OK.

The expected crating date is September 2021. This would mean I should be expecting it at my doorstep maybe later in September 2021.

Now I will just patiently wait and use the time to better educate myself about the blueprints (plans) that I have also ordered and should be coming anytime soon.

HomebuiltHelp RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD

📅 May 11, 2021 📁 Planning ⏱ 1 hour (Research & Education)
[Photo: RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD by HomebuiltHelp — placeholder]

RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD by HomebuiltHelp.

While my tail kit arrives, I decided to spend my time educating myself about the basics of construction, tools needed, how to use these tools, and most importantly the tail kit itself. For that purpose, I ordered the RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD and the Preview Plans for RV7 from Van's.

When the Preview Plans arrive I will get a bird's-eye view of the entire project and also some specific details that might help me in my planning and using this time before the kit comes. As for now, the DVD arrived and I managed to watch it.

The DVD started off by talking about basic tools needed, their usage, aluminum sheet metal types, rivets, riveting, back-riveting, dimpling and countersinking, and rivet removal. These were not just talked about in technical detail but were demonstrated step by step.

After that, the actual RV7 Rudder was built and shown in steps. This really helped me grasp what was coming. I had a lingering sense of being lost in complexities. The DVD demo of the rudder assembly has cleared most of it and now I can at least know what to expect and where to begin.

Tags: EAA homebuilt rudder rv7 workshop

Ordered the Empennage Kit (Tail)

📅 April 26, 2021 📁 Planning

Today, I placed my order for the RV-7 tail kit. The option I chose was with the electric trim and not the manual. I thought it would just be easier to wire an electric cable at the end than to work with cables and pulleys for the manual trim.

I want to build the plane with less intricacy as I have some time constraints being in the USA. I also realize that I will not be compromising on the quality of the build, as this is going to be the plane I will be flying across the continents to Pakistan.

[Photo: Email confirming the order — placeholder]

Email confirming the order and 25% payment required.

And so I paid the 25% on it, which was $620. Now the time to wait — about 4 months is what they said. These large wait times are supposedly due to the large number of people stuck at home during COVID and building planes.

In that regard, I also must start thinking about ordering my wing or the fuselage kit soon since the wait times on those are about 6 months (plus shipping).

I also need to now form my little workshop, which is from now on called the "Uran Khatola Factory (UKF)". I am looking to move into a house that has a nice garage, as I will not only need the space but my air tools will make noise. I don't want to disturb the neighbours.

More about the Van's RV-7: vansaircraft.com/rv-7/

[Photo: RV7 landing in sunset — placeholder]

An RV-7 landing in sunset. Photo from Van's Website.

Tags: orders planning

Done. Here's what was built:

**Structure:**
```
website/
  style.css
  index.html        ← home page
  log.html          ← full build log index
  about.html        ← about Bibrak & the project
  posts/            ← 13 posts, all imported from WordPress
    attended-fiberglass-workshop.html     (Mar 25, 2026)

    horizontal-stabilizer-update-2.html   (Mar 19, 2022)

    empennage-inventoried.html            (Oct 27, 2021)
    finished-the-practice-flap-kit.html   (Oct 20, 2021)
    became-a-pilot-today.html             (Sep 25, 2021)
    a-beauty-came-to-kbmg.html            (Aug 30, 2021)
    marks-rv7.html                        (Aug 30, 2021)
    getting-to-know-tools.html            (Jul 11, 2021)
    update-on-empennage-crating-date.html (May 12, 2021)
    homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd.html (May 11, 2021)
    ordered-the-empennage-kit.html        (Apr 26, 2021)
```

**Design choices:**
- Cream/parchment background (`#f5f0e8`), dark navy text, deep red accent — easy on the eyes, like old aviation manuals
- Courier New for nav/headers/meta (typewriter feel), Georgia serif for body text (readable)
- Fixed 860px max-width with a sidebar — classic builder-site layout
- Sidebar collapses below main content on mobile/tablet (responsive grid)
- Build hours table on every page, prev/next post navigation on each post
- All image slots are dashed placeholder boxes — ready to swap in real photos

To preview it locally: `cd website && python3 -m http.server 8080` then open `http://localhost:8080`.

input.content: # Uran Khatola Factory — Website Static website chronicling the build of a Van's RV-8 homebuilt aircraft. **Live WordPress (source of truth for content):** https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/ **Owner:** Bibrak Qamar Chandio (bibrakc@gmail.com) ## Stack Pure HTML + CSS. No framework, no build step, no JavaScript. Host anywhere: GitHub Pages, Netlify, S3 static hosting, etc. ## Structure ``` website/ style.css # single stylesheet index.html # home page log.html # build log index (all posts, chronological) about.html # about the builder and project posts/ # one .html file per blog post img/ # photos go here (currently empty, placeholders in HTML) ``` ## Running Locally ```bash cd website python3 -m http.server 8080 # open http://localhost:8080 ``` ## Adding a New Post 1. Copy any existing file in `posts/` as a template. 2. Update the title, date, category, body content, and prev/next nav links. 3. Add the entry to `log.html` (in the correct year section). 4. Add it to the "Recent Posts" sidebar on `index.html` if it's one of the 5 most recent. 5. Replace `img-placeholder` divs with real `` tags once photos are available. ## Design Principles - Vintage 2000s builder-site aesthetic: cream background, dark navy text, deep red accent - Courier New for nav/meta/headers, Georgia serif for body - Max width 860px, two-column layout (main + sidebar), sidebar collapses on mobile - No animations, no gradients, no JavaScript — flat and fast - Image placeholders use a dashed box; swap with `` when ready

input.content:
# Agent Context — Uran Khatola Factory Website This file gives an AI agent everything it needs to work on this project without prior conversation history. ## Project Summary A static personal website for Bibrak Qamar Chandio, who is building a **Van's RV-8 homebuilt aircraft** in his garage in Cupertino, California. The site chronicles the build with blog-style posts, a build hours tracker, and links to social media. - **WordPress source:** https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/ - **Owner/author:** Bibrak Qamar Chandio (`bibrakc@gmail.com`) - **Social:** YouTube, Facebook (`urankhatolafactory`), Instagram (`man_in_30s`) ## Tech Stack - Pure HTML5 + CSS3. No JavaScript, no framework, no build tool. - Single stylesheet: `website/style.css` - All pages are self-contained `.html` files. ## Design System | Token | Value | Usage | |---|---|---| | `--bg` | `#f5f0e8` | Page background (cream/parchment) | | `--bg-alt` | `#ede8dc` | Sidebar, intro boxes, alt sections | | `--border` | `#b0a898` | All borders and dividers | | `--text` | `#1a1a2e` | Body text (dark navy) | | `--text-muted` | `#5a5a6e` | Dates, captions, meta | | `--accent` | `#8b1a1a` | Deep red — borders, hover, highlights | | `--link` | `#1a3a6e` | Link color | | `--nav-bg` | `#1a1a2e` | Nav and footer background | | `--max-width` | `860px` | Content max width | **Fonts:** Courier New (nav, meta, headers, monospace elements) + Georgia serif (body text). **Aesthetic:** Vintage 2000s homebuilder website. No animations, no gradients, flat layout. **Responsive:** Two-column grid (main + 220px sidebar). Sidebar stacks below main at ≤680px. ## File Map ``` website/ style.css index.html home — intro, 5 recent posts, hours sidebar log.html full chronological post index grouped by year about.html about Bibrak and the RV-8 project img/ photos (empty — placeholders used in HTML) posts/ attended-fiberglass-workshop.html 2026-03-25 Uncategorized back-to-building.html 2026-02-19 Uncategorized horizontal-stabilizer-update-2.html 2022-03-19 Empennage (49.3 hrs) horizontal-stabilizer-update-1.html 2021-12-01 Empennage (14.6 hrs) empennage-inventoried.html 2021-10-27 Empennage finished-the-practice-flap-kit.html 2021-10-20 Planning became-a-pilot-today.html 2021-09-25 Flying & Planes a-beauty-came-to-kbmg.html 2021-08-30 Flying & Planes marks-rv7.html 2021-08-30 Uncategorized getting-to-know-tools.html 2021-07-11 Planning update-on-empennage-crating-date.html 2021-05-12 Planning homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd.html 2021-05-11 Planning ordered-the-empennage-kit.html 2021-04-26 Planning ``` ## HTML Conventions ### Page skeleton (top-level pages) ```html ``` ### Nav — mark the active page ```html
  • Build Log
    • ``` ### Post header block ```html

    Post Title

    📅 Month DD, YYYY 📁 Category ⏱ X hours
    ``` ### Image placeholder (swap with real img when photo available) ```html
    [Photo: description — placeholder]

    Caption text.

    ``` ### Real image (once photos exist) ```html

    Caption text.

    ``` ### Post prev/next nav ```html
    ← Previous Title Next Title →
    ``` ### Sidebar (same on every page — update hours when they change) ```html

    Build Hours

    Workshop10
    Research & Education15
    Empennage63.9
    Total88.9

    Recent Posts

    ``` ## How to Add a New Post 1. Copy `posts/attended-fiberglass-workshop.html` as a template. 2. Set the filename as `posts/kebab-case-title.html`. 3. Update: ``, `<h1>`, post-meta (date, category, hours), body content, tags, post-nav links. 4. Add an entry to `log.html` in the correct year `<ul class="post-list">`. 5. If it's in the 5 most recent, update the "Recent Posts" sidebar in `index.html` and all post pages. 6. Update build hours in the sidebar table on all pages if hours changed. ## Current Build Hours (update when new posts are added) | Category | Hours | |---|---| | Workshop | 10 | | Research & Education | 15 | | Empennage | 63.9 | | **Total** | **88.9** | ## External Links Used in the Site - Van's RV-8: https://www.vansaircraft.com/rv-8/ - EAA: https://www.eaa.org/ - HomebuiltHelp: https://homebuilthelp.com - YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCkw7kZ2sxUoKswTp_emBPEg - Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/urankhatolafactory - Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/man_in_30s/ - LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/bibrak-qamar-chandio-6444b51b/

FileWrite.content:
# Agent Context — Uran Khatola Factory Website This file gives an AI agent everything it needs to work on this project without prior conversation history. ## Project Summary A static personal website for Bibrak Qamar Chandio, who is building a **Van's RV-8 homebuilt aircraft** in his garage in Cupertino, California. The site chronicles the build with blog-style posts, a build hours tracker, and links to social media. - **WordPress source:** https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/ - **Owner/author:** Bibrak Qamar Chandio (`bibrakc@gmail.com`) - **Social:** YouTube, Facebook (`urankhatolafactory`), Instagram (`man_in_30s`) ## Tech Stack - Pure HTML5 + CSS3. No JavaScript, no framework, no build tool. - Single stylesheet: `website/style.css` - All pages are self-contained `.html` files. ## Design System | Token | Value | Usage | |---|---|---| | `--bg` | `#f5f0e8` | Page background (cream/parchment) | | `--bg-alt` | `#ede8dc` | Sidebar, intro boxes, alt sections | | `--border` | `#b0a898` | All borders and dividers | | `--text` | `#1a1a2e` | Body text (dark navy) | | `--text-muted` | `#5a5a6e` | Dates, captions, meta | | `--accent` | `#8b1a1a` | Deep red — borders, hover, highlights | | `--link` | `#1a3a6e` | Link color | | `--nav-bg` | `#1a1a2e` | Nav and footer background | | `--max-width` | `860px` | Content max width | **Fonts:** Courier New (nav, meta, headers, monospace elements) + Georgia serif (body text). **Aesthetic:** Vintage 2000s homebuilder website. No animations, no gradients, flat layout. **Responsive:** Two-column grid (main + 220px sidebar). Sidebar stacks below main at ≤680px. ## File Map ``` website/ style.css index.html home — intro, 5 recent posts, hours sidebar log.html full chronological post index grouped by year about.html about Bibrak and the RV-8 project img/ photos (empty — placeholders used in HTML) posts/ attended-fiberglass-workshop.html 2026-03-25 Uncategorized back-to-building.html 2026-02-19 Uncategorized horizontal-stabilizer-update-2.html 2022-03-19 Empennage (49.3 hrs) horizontal-stabilizer-update-1.html 2021-12-01 Empennage (14.6 hrs) empennage-inventoried.html 2021-10-27 Empennage finished-the-practice-flap-kit.html 2021-10-20 Planning became-a-pilot-today.html 2021-09-25 Flying & Planes a-beauty-came-to-kbmg.html 2021-08-30 Flying & Planes marks-rv7.html 2021-08-30 Uncategorized getting-to-know-tools.html 2021-07-11 Planning update-on-empennage-crating-date.html 2021-05-12 Planning homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd.html 2021-05-11 Planning ordered-the-empennage-kit.html 2021-04-26 Planning ``` ## HTML Conventions ### Page skeleton (top-level pages) ```html ``` ### Nav — mark the active page ```html
  • Build Log
    • ``` ### Post header block ```html

    Post Title

    📅 Month DD, YYYY 📁 Category ⏱ X hours
    ``` ### Image placeholder (swap with real img when photo available) ```html
    [Photo: description — placeholder]

    Caption text.

    ``` ### Real image (once photos exist) ```html

    Caption text.

    ``` ### Post prev/next nav ```html
    ← Previous Title Next Title →
    ``` ### Sidebar (same on every page — update hours when they change) ```html

    Build Hours

    Workshop10
    Research & Education15
    Empennage63.9
    Total88.9

    Recent Posts

    ``` ## How to Add a New Post 1. Copy `posts/attended-fiberglass-workshop.html` as a template. 2. Set the filename as `posts/kebab-case-title.html`. 3. Update: ``, `<h1>`, post-meta (date, category, hours), body content, tags, post-nav links. 4. Add an entry to `log.html` in the correct year `<ul class="post-list">`. 5. If it's in the 5 most recent, update the "Recent Posts" sidebar in `index.html` and all post pages. 6. Update build hours in the sidebar table on all pages if hours changed. ## Current Build Hours (update when new posts are added) | Category | Hours | |---|---| | Workshop | 10 | | Research & Education | 15 | | Empennage | 63.9 | | **Total** | **88.9** | ## External Links Used in the Site - Van's RV-8: https://www.vansaircraft.com/rv-8/ - EAA: https://www.eaa.org/ - HomebuiltHelp: https://homebuilthelp.com - YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCkw7kZ2sxUoKswTp_emBPEg - Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/urankhatolafactory - Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/man_in_30s/ - LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/bibrak-qamar-chandio-6444b51b/

content.data:
Three files created at the project root:

- **README.md** — stack, structure, how to run locally, how to add a post, design principles. Standard project README.

- **TASKS.md** — organized backlog: In Progress, Up Next, Backlog (content + design + technical), and Completed. Tracks what's done and what's next.

- **AGENT_CONTEXT.md** — the most important one for AI agents. Contains everything needed to work on this project cold: design token table, full file map with dates/categories, HTML conventions with copy-paste snippets for post headers, image placeholders, nav, sidebar, step-by-step instructions for adding a new post, current build hours, and all external links. Any agent dropped into this repo can read that file and immediately know what to do and how to do it consistently.

# Back to Building!

Posted on [February 19, 2026][9] by [bibrak][10]

After about four years, I am back at building a plane. Life happened and I had to move four times, which impacted my progress on the RV-7 aircraft. I sold that empennage kit and my tools. I have since moved to Cupertino, California. This time, I am going to start building a Van’s RV-8. I thought if I were going to build a plane, it had better be a tandem. The RV-8 has a tandem seating arrangement where the pilot and the passenger/second pilot sit one behind the other, in a single-file line along the aircraft’s centerline, rather than side by side.

Vans RV8 (Picture from Van’s [RV8 website page][11])

The Van’s RV-8 was designed by Richard VanGrunsven. It is fully aerobatic-capable, able to perform loops, rolls, and other maneuvers. It is offered as a kit. You get thousands of small parts, blue print drawings, and build/assembly instructions.

[img_6929]Some empennage parts as I inventory them.

I have started inventorying the empennage kit parts and setting up my workshop, which for now is mostly in the living room. The empennage parts are not that large, so the living room should suffice until I move on to the next section, which will be either the fuselage or the wings.

content.data: 2880px wide — needs constraining. The fix is a global CSS rule for all `img` tags, not just inside `.post-body`:

input.__tool_use_purpose: Add global img max-width rule to style.css
input.oldStr: * { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }
input.newStr:
* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }

img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; display: block; }

Good! Now you see the banner was designed to have white background. Lets make the site white in the header and not navy blue. And instead of navy blue in the rest of the site we use hard green reflecting pakistani flag (although we will use more darker green than the Pakistani flag since we are replacing the navy blue). Red lines and uage is fine for now. I think off-white whereever it is is also fine. Let me know what you think

Good call — the white banner on dark navy was clashing. Here's my thinking:

- **Header/footer background:** white (`#ffffff`) so the banner logo sits naturally
- **Nav bar:** dark forest green (`#1a4a2e`) — darker than Pakistani flag green, readable, not garish
- **`--nav-bg` replacement:** same dark green everywhere it was navy
- **`--text`:** keep dark (near-black) for readability — swapping navy body text to green would hurt legibility
- **`--link`:** deep green (`#1a4a2e`) instead of navy blue
- **Accent red:** stays as-is

That gives you green + white + red — a nod to the flag without being literal about it.

- **Header:** white background, green title text, red bottom border — banner logo now sits naturally

hmmm, now I don't like the off-white. Do we also convert it to white or you have other ideas?

The off-white (`#f5f0e8` / `#ede8dc`) was originally there to give that aged-paper vintage feel. Now that the header is white and the nav is green, the off-white background feels inconsistent rather than intentional.

My take: go full white for the page background, but keep a **very light green tint** (`#f0f5f1`) for the alternate areas (sidebar sections, intro box, post list backgrounds). That way:
- Clean, modern-ish white canvas
- The green tint ties the alt areas to the nav/footer color family
- Still has visual separation without the yellowed-paper look

Want me to do that, or just go pure white everywhere?

nice, we may come back to it later. But now lets organize our folders. the post and img folders will become a mess as new posts and logs of media are put there. Lets organize them by year, have a folder hierarchy by year for both post and img. Then for each post we have a separate folder in its respective img folder hierarchy to not mess up with other post's images (and videos). We keep the common media of our webite in the parent img folder with a folder common. Just my thinking but you also brainstrom on it and let me know what is your plan then we implement it

Good thinking. Here's my proposed structure and reasoning:

```
website/
  posts/
    2021/
    2022/
    2026/
  img/
    common/          ← site-wide: banner, logo, placeholder art
    2021/
    2022/
    2026/
      back-to-building/
        rv8a-wolf-over-lighthouse.jpg
        img_6929.jpg
      attended-fiberglass-workshop/
        img_7085.jpg
```

**Where I agree with you:**
- Year folders for posts — clean, chronological, easy to navigate
- Per-post image folders — no filename collisions, easy to know what belongs where
- `common/` for site-wide assets

**One thing I'd adjust from your proposal:**
- Rather than `img/2026/back-to-building/`, I'd mirror the posts path exactly: `img/2026/back-to-building/`. This means the relative path from a post to its images is always a predictable `../../img/YEAR/POST-SLUG/filename.jpg` — no guessing.

**The only tradeoff:** all existing HTML links need updating (13 posts move, image paths change). That's mechanical work I can do with a script.

**Proposed final layout:**
```
posts/2021/ordered-the-empennage-kit.html
posts/2021/homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd.html
... (all 2021 posts)
posts/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2.html
posts/2026/back-to-building.html
posts/2026/attended-fiberglass-workshop.html

img/common/cropped-uuran-khatola-factory-01-copy.jpg  (banner)
img/2026/back-to-building/rv8a-wolf-over-lighthouse.jpg
img/2026/back-to-building/img_6929.jpg
img/2026/attended-fiberglass-workshop/img_7085.jpg
```

From a post like `posts/2026/back-to-building.html`, the banner would be `../../img/common/banner.jpg` and its own images `../../img/2026/back-to-building/photo.jpg`. Consistent and predictable.

Does this match your vision, or do you want to tweak anything before I implement?
yes

input.command:
cd /home/bchandio/work/workspace/uran-khatola-factory/website && \
python3 -c "
import os, re
errors = []
for root, dirs, files in os.walk('.'):
    for f in files:
        if not f.endswith('.html'): continue
        path = os.path.join(root, f)
        html = open(path).read()
        for href in re.findall(r'href=\"([^\"]+\.html)\"', html):
            if href.startswith('http'): continue
            target = os.path.normpath(os.path.join(root, href))
            if not os.path.exists(target):
                errors.append(f'{path}: broken -> {href}')
for e in errors: print(e)
print(f'Done. {len(errors)} broken links.')
"

ok now tell me how do we count and update that hours loged table that we have on the upper right side?

Right now the hours table is **hardcoded** in the sidebar of every single HTML file. That means to update one number you'd have to edit 16 files — which is painful and error-prone.

**The real options:**

**Option A — Keep it manual, but centralize with a shell script**
Write a script: `update-hours.sh 10 15 63.9` that does a find-and-replace across all files. Simple, no new tech, fits the "no JS" philosophy.

**Option B — Single source of truth file + build script**
Keep hours in one `hours.json` or `hours.txt`, run a script to regenerate the sidebar HTML across all files. More robust but adds a tiny build step.

**Option C — JavaScript include (one small exception to the no-JS rule)**
A tiny `<script src="../../hours.js"></script>` that writes the table. Update one file, all pages reflect it instantly. No build step needed.

**Option D — Server-side include (only if hosted on Apache/Nginx)**
Not relevant for GitHub Pages/Netlify static hosting.

---

My recommendation: **Option A** — a simple shell script. It fits the static site philosophy, no new dependencies, and you only run it when hours change. Something like:

```bash
./update-hours.sh 10 15 75.4
# updates all 16 HTML files in one shot
```

Want me to build that?

\s*

Build Hours

.*?
\s*', re.DOTALL) files = [] for root, dirs, fs in os.walk('.'): for f in fs: if f.endswith('.html') and f != 'index.html': files.append(os.path.join(root, f)) for path in files: content = open(path).read() new_content = pattern.sub('\n ', content) if new_content != content: open(path, 'w').write(new_content) print(f'Updated: {path}') "

content.data: Done. Now — back to the update mechanism. Since the table only lives in one file now (`index.html`), the problem is much simpler. A small script that takes the four numbers as arguments and patches the file is all you need. Want me to build that?

wait, now I am thinking that for any new post we have a tag (or something a section or a liner somewhere) that says time logged x hours. Then we pick that x and the post's category (or the hours themselvs have some category) and add those hours to the table

That's a clean idea. Each post already has the hours in the `post-meta` line — we just need to make it machine-readable. Here's my proposal:

Each post has a single line in the `<head>` as a meta tag:

```html
<meta name="build-hours" content="16" category="research-education">
```

Then a script `update-hours.sh` scans all post HTML files, reads those meta tags, sums by category, and rewrites the table in `index.html`. You run it after adding any new post.

The categories map like this:

| `category` value | Table row |
|---|---|
| `workshop` | Workshop |
| `research-education` | Research & Education |
| `empennage` | Empennage |

So the workflow for a new post is:
1. Add the post HTML with the meta tag
2. Run `./update-hours.sh`
3. `index.html` hours table updates automatically

Simple, no JS, no database, fits the static site. Want me to implement this — add the meta tags to existing posts and write the script?

yes and when we start building wings we add category in that first post and the script sees a new category then it adds a new row in the table. Also make sure that the hours logged are also displaced to the readers

input.__tool_use_purpose: Write update-hours.sh script
input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3 """ update-hours.sh Scans all post HTML files for tags, sums hours by category, and rewrites the hours table in index.html. Usage: python3 update-hours.py Run this from the website/ directory after adding or editing any post. """ import os import re WEBSITE_DIR = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)) INDEX_HTML = os.path.join(WEBSITE_DIR, "index.html") # Human-readable labels for each category key CATEGORY_LABELS = { "workshop": "Workshop", "research-education": "Research & Education", "empennage": "Empennage", "wings": "Wings", "fuselage": "Fuselage", "firewall-forward": "Firewall Forward", "finishing": "Finishing", } # Preferred display order (unknown categories appended at end) CATEGORY_ORDER = [ "workshop", "research-education", "empennage", "wings", "fuselage", "firewall-forward", "finishing", ] def collect_hours(): totals = {} meta_re = re.compile( r'{label}{hrs_str}\n" total_str = str(int(grand_total)) if grand_total == int(grand_total) else str(grand_total) rows += f" Total{total_str}" return f" \n{rows}\n
" def update_index(table_html): content = open(INDEX_HTML).read() new_content = re.sub( r'.*?
', table_html, content, flags=re.DOTALL ) if new_content == content: print("No changes needed.") return open(INDEX_HTML, "w").write(new_content) print("index.html hours table updated.") if __name__ == "__main__": totals = collect_hours() if not totals: print("No build-hours meta tags found in any post.") else: print("Hours found:") for cat, hrs in totals.items(): label = CATEGORY_LABELS.get(cat, cat) print(f" {label}: {hrs}") table_html = build_table_html(totals) update_index(table_html)

content.data:
All done. Here's how it works going forward:

**Adding a new post:**
1. Add to the `<head>`: `<meta name="build-hours" content="X" data-category="empennage">`
2. Run `python3 update-hours.py` from the `website/` directory
3. `index.html` table updates automatically

**New category (e.g. Wings):** just use `data-category="wings"` in the post — the script sees it, adds a new row automatically in the right order.

**Hours are also shown to readers** in the post-meta line (the ⏱ span) on each post that has time logged.

now lets create a new special categoty that is displayed in the table separately after the Total sum but is not counted as Sum. That category is something like Flying and Social. You would have seen posts of some planes, me visiting other builder's workshops.

Good idea — these are logged activities but not build hours, so they shouldn't inflate the build total. I'll add a `social-flying` category that renders below a separator after the Total row.

First, update the script:

input.__tool_use_purpose: Update update-hours.py to support non-build categories shown separately

# Categories excluded from the build total (shown separately below) NON_BUILD_CATEGORIES = {"social-flying"} def build_table_html(totals): # Order known categories first, then any new ones alphabetically build_cats = [c for c in CATEGORY_ORDER if c in totals and c not in NON_BUILD_CATEGORIES] build_cats += sorted(c for c in totals if c not in CATEGORY_ORDER and c not in NON_BUILD_CATEGORIES) extra_cats = [c for c in totals if c in NON_BUILD_CATEGORIES] rows = "" grand_total = 0 for cat in build_cats: hrs = totals[cat] grand_total += hrs label = CATEGORY_LABELS.get(cat, cat.replace("-", " ").title()) hrs_str = str(int(hrs)) if hrs == int(hrs) else str(hrs) rows += f" {label}{hrs_str}\n" total_str = str(int(grand_total)) if grand_total == int(grand_total) else str(grand_total) rows += f" Total{total_str}" for cat in extra_cats: hrs = totals[cat] label = CATEGORY_LABELS.get(cat, cat.replace("-", " ").title()) hrs_str = str(int(hrs)) if hrs == int(hrs) else str(hrs) rows += f"\n " rows += f"\n {label}{hrs_str}" return f" \n{rows}\n
"

Now — before I add meta tags to those posts, what hours should I log for each? The posts are:

- **Became a Pilot Today** (Sep 25, 2021) — checkride day
- **A Beauty Came to KBMG** (Aug 30, 2021) — saw an RV-7 at the airport
- **Mark's RV7** (Aug 30, 2021) — visited Mark's workshop

Do you have hours in mind, or should I leave them at 0 for now and you fill them in later?
I see you have pre populated the labels for the categories. So, does it mean that if we add a new category then we need to update that label array?

No — the label array is optional. If a category key isn't in `CATEGORY_LABELS`, the script falls back to auto-formatting the key:

```python
label = CATEGORY_LABELS.get(cat, cat.replace("-", " ").title())
```

So `wings` → "Wings", `firewall-forward` → "Firewall Forward" automatically. The pre-populated labels are just there to handle cases where the auto-format wouldn't look right — like `research-education` would become "Research-Education" without it.

You only need to add to `CATEGORY_LABELS` if the auto-formatted version looks wrong. For most single-word categories like `empennage`, `wings`, `fuselage` — you never need to touch it.

content.data: Two separate sidebar sections now — **Build Hours** with the total, and **Other Hours** below it with its own green header. Flying & Social sits cleanly outside the build count.
now in the future if I add a new category XYZ how will the script know that it belongs to Build Hours or Other Hours?

Right now it uses a hardcoded set `NON_BUILD_CATEGORIES = {"social-flying"}` — so any new category defaults to Build Hours unless you add it to that set in the script.

That's a friction point. Better approach: let the post itself declare which bucket it belongs to with a second attribute:

```html
<meta name="build-hours" content="2" data-category="social-flying" data-bucket="other">
```

Default (no `data-bucket`) = build hours. `data-bucket="other"` = Other Hours. The script reads it and routes accordingly — no need to ever touch the script for new categories.

Want me to update the script and the existing `social-flying` posts to use this?
yes and lets also not name it Other Hours, comeup with a good label. Like Non-Build Hours? Or any other idea you have?

A few options:

- **Beyond the Build** — has a nice ring, covers flying, socializing, visiting other builders
- **Field & Flight** — evocative but too narrow
- **Non-Build Hours** — clear but clinical
- **Around Aviation** — captures the spirit well

I'd go with **Beyond the Build** — it's descriptive, fits the vintage personal-site tone, and naturally covers anything that's aviation-adjacent but not wrenching.

content.data:
Working. Now for any future category, the rule is simple — just set `data-bucket` in the post meta tag:

- `data-bucket="build"` (or omit it) → goes into Build Hours + Total
- `data-bucket="other"` → goes into Beyond the Build, excluded from Total

No script changes ever needed for new categories.

input.command:
cd /home/bchandio/work/workspace/uran-khatola-factory/website

BASE="https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads"

download() {
  local dir=$1; shift
  mkdir -p "$dir"
  for url in "$@"; do
    fname=$(basename "$url")
    curl -s -o "$dir/$fname" "$url" && echo "  $fname"
  done
}

content.data: All downloaded. Now I need to fetch the actual WordPress post HTML to understand the order and captions of images, then update each post HTML. Let me fetch the raw HTML for each post to get captions:

input.__tool_use_purpose: Get image order and captions from WordPress posts
input.command: for slug_url in \ "horizontal-stabilizer-update-2|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2022/03/19/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/" \ "horizontal-stabilizer-update-1|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/12/01/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/" \ "empennage-inventoried|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/10/27/empennage-inventoried/" \ "finished-the-practice-flap-kit|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/10/20/finished-the-practice-flap-kit/" \ "became-a-pilot-today|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/09/25/became-a-pilot-today/" \ "a-beauty-came-to-kbmg|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/08/30/a-beauty-came-to-kbmg/" \ "marks-rv7|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/08/30/marks-rv7/" \ "getting-to-know-tools|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/07/11/getting-to-know-tools/" \ "update-on-empennage-crating-date|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/05/12/update-on-empennage-crating-date/" \ "homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/05/11/homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd/" \ "ordered-the-empennage-kit|https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/2021/04/26/ordered-the-empennage-kit-tail/"; do slug="${slug_url%%|*}" url="${slug_url##*|}" echo "=== $slug ===" curl -s "$url" | python3 -c " import sys, re html = sys.stdin.read() # Extract img src (filename only) and figcaption pairs blocks = re.findall(r']*>(.*?)', html, re.DOTALL) for b in blocks: imgs = re.findall(r'data-orig-file=\"[^\"]*?/([^/\"]+\.(jpg|jpeg|png|gif|webp))\"', b, re.I) cap = re.search(r']*>(.*?)', b, re.DOTALL) cap_text = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', '', cap.group(1)).strip() if cap else '' for img in imgs: print(f' {img[0]} | {cap_text}') " done

content.data: I have all the images and captions. Now I'll update each post HTML, replacing placeholders with real images. I'll do them in batches:

input.__tool_use_purpose: Update all post HTML files with real images using Python
input.command:
cd /home/bchandio/work/workspace/uran-khatola-factory/website && python3 << 'EOF' import re def img_tag(path, alt="", caption=""): html = f' \n' if caption: html += f'

{caption}

\n' return html def replace_placeholder(content, placeholder_text, img_html): old = f'
[{placeholder_text}]
\n' return content.replace(old, img_html, 1) def write(path, content): open(path, 'w').write(content) print(f"Updated: {path}") BASE = "../../img" # ── ordered-the-empennage-kit ────────────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/ordered-the-empennage-kit.html" c = open(p).read() c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Email confirming the order — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/ordered-the-empennage-kit/tail-order.png", "Order confirmation email", "Email confirming the order and 25% payment required.")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: RV7 landing in sunset — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/ordered-the-empennage-kit/rv6-brazil1-2880x1601-homeherobanner.jpg", "RV landing in sunset", "An RV-7 landing in sunset. Photo from Van's Website.")) write(p, c) # ── homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd ───────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd.html" c = open(p).read() c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD by HomebuiltHelp — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd/whatsapp-image-2021-05-11-at-23.45.48.jpeg", "RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD", "RV7 Rudder Workshop DVD by HomebuiltHelp.")) write(p, c) # ── update-on-empennage-crating-date ────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/update-on-empennage-crating-date.html" c = open(p).read() c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Crate date letter from Van's — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/update-on-empennage-crating-date/whatsapp-image-2021-05-12-at-00.01.45.jpeg", "Crate date letter from Van's", "Crate Date Letter from Van's.")) write(p, c) # ── getting-to-know-tools ────────────────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/getting-to-know-tools.html" c = open(p).read() imgs = ["img_6911","img_6925","img_6922","img_6921","img_6923","img_6920", "img_6908","img_6909","img_6910","img_6907","img_6901","img_6903","img_6898","img_6896"] gallery = "".join(f' \n' for i in imgs) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Richard showing aluminum edge smoothing — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/getting-to-know-tools/img_6911.jpg", "Richard showing aluminum edge smoothing", "Richard showing me how to smooth out aluminum edges using an air grinder.")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Russ's tools at his workshop — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/getting-to-know-tools/img_6925.jpg", "Russ's tools at his workshop", "Russ's tools at his workshop.")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: More tools — placeholder", "".join(f' \n' for i in ["img_6922","img_6921","img_6923","img_6920","img_6908","img_6909", "img_6910","img_6907","img_6901","img_6903","img_6898","img_6896"])) write(p, c) # ── marks-rv7 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/marks-rv7.html" c = open(p).read() c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Mark's RV7 with cowl open — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/marks-rv7/img_7696.jpg", "Mark's RV7 with cowl open", "Mark opened the cowl to show me the details of the engine installation.")) # Add remaining photos as a gallery after the caption extra = "".join(f' \n' for i in ["img_7708","img_7707","img_7705","img_7704","img_7699","img_7698","img_7692"]) c = c.replace('

Mark opened the cowl to show me the details of the engine installation.

\n', f'

Mark opened the cowl to show me the details of the engine installation.

\n{extra}') write(p, c) # ── a-beauty-came-to-kbmg ───────────────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/a-beauty-came-to-kbmg.html" c = open(p).read() gallery = "".join(f' \n' for i in ["img_7841","img_7843","img_7846","img_7845"]) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Van's RV-7 at KBMG — placeholder", gallery) write(p, c) # ── became-a-pilot-today ────────────────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/became-a-pilot-today.html" c = open(p).read() c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Checkride pass at BMG Aviation — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/became-a-pilot-today/img_8651.jpg", "Checkride pass at BMG Aviation", "Dave Ellis (on the left) helped me push towards it. Jim Jacobi was my examiner (black shirt on the right).") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/became-a-pilot-today/img_8650.jpg", "Cessna 150 N66591")) write(p, c) # ── finished-the-practice-flap-kit ──────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/finished-the-practice-flap-kit.html" c = open(p).read() c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Completed practice flap — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/finished-the-practice-flap-kit/img_8520.jpg", "Completed practice flap", "Completed practice flap.")) process_imgs = "".join(f' \n' for i in ["img_8519","img_8517","img_7988","img_7990","img_8399","img_8402","img_8400","img_8407"]) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Practice flap build process — placeholder", process_imgs) write(p, c) # ── empennage-inventoried ───────────────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/empennage-inventoried.html" c = open(p).read() gallery = (img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/empennage-inventoried/img_9161.jpg", "Empennage kit arrival") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/empennage-inventoried/6dc4f43c-43c9-4baa-bd80-133b1507131f.jpg", "Empennage kit parts") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/empennage-inventoried/img_9183.jpg", "Empennage inventory")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Empennage kit arrival — placeholder", gallery) write(p, c) # ── horizontal-stabilizer-update-1 ──────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1.html" c = open(p).read() c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Initial smoothing of spar reinforcement — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9225.jpg", "Initial smoothing of spar reinforcement", "Initial smoothing of the spar reinforcement.") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9226.jpg", "Spar reinforcement") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9224.jpg", "Spar reinforcement primed")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: 3M wheel dents — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9290.jpg", "3M wheel dents on spar", "The 3M wheel and my mishandling caused those little dents.") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9248.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9250.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9251.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9253.jpg")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Rear spar assembly — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9291.jpg", "Rear spar assembly", "Rear spar assembly and double-checking those unusual holes like the #21.") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9292.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9294.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9310.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9311.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9671.jpg", "Front spar", "The front spar of the horizontal stabilizer was tricky to understand first.") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1/img_9672.jpg")) write(p, c) # ── horizontal-stabilizer-update-2 ──────────────────────────────────────── p = "posts/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2.html" c = open(p).read() c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Completed horizontal stabilizer — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_2882-1.jpg", "Horizontal stabilizer complete", "Horizontal Stabilizer complete.")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Ruined rib — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0165.jpg", "A ruined rib", "A ruined rib.") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0167.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0316.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0317.jpg")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Doubler to fix edge clearance — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0833.jpg", "Doubler to fix edge clearance", "The doubler to fix edge clearance.") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0459.jpg") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0460.jpg")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Fixing the dimpling hole — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0891.jpg", "Fixing dimpling hole", "Fixing the hole caused by the dimpling mistake.") + img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_0892.jpg")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Leading edge of HS-707 protruding — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_1721.jpg", "Leading edge of HS-707 protruding", "The leading edge of HS-707 protruding.")) c = replace_placeholder(c, "Photo: Rear spar misalignment — placeholder", img_tag(f"{BASE}/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2/img_2892.jpg", "Rear spar misalignment", "Rear spar misalignment.") + import os, re

content.data:
It's stale — copied from WordPress. The actual counts and categories have changed. Two options:

1. **Remove it** — the Build Log page already organizes by year, and categories are shown on each post. It's redundant.
2. **Update it** to reflect the real categories and counts from the actual posts.

The real breakdown across all 13 posts is:
- Empennage: 3
- Flying & Social: 3
- Planning: 5
- Research & Education: 2

Which do you prefer — remove it or update it?
we can update it but you see it also has link to the category. We don't have a category wide page that indexes and list the posts

Two options:

1. **Remove the links, just show counts** — simple, no dead links, honest about what the site is.
2. **Add anchor sections to `log.html` per category** — filter the log by category so clicking "Empennage (3)" jumps to a section listing only empennage posts.

Option 2 is more useful for readers and not much work — `log.html` already groups by year, we just add category anchors. But it means reorganizing `log.html` from year-grouped to category-grouped, or having both.

My recommendation: keep `log.html` year-grouped (that's the builder-site tradition), and make the category links simply filter by scrolling to an anchor that has all posts of that category listed. Essentially a second view at the bottom of `log.html`.

Or simplest: just remove the links and show plain text counts. Clean, no dead links, no extra pages needed.

What do you prefer?
show me how the option 2 with both year and category will look like

Here's how `log.html` would look with both views:

LOG_HTML = os.path.join(WEBSITE_DIR, "log.html") # ── Post metadata collection ─────────────────────────────────────────────── def collect_posts(): """Return list of dicts with title, path (relative to website/), date, category, hours.""" posts = [] title_re = re.compile(r'

content.data: Done.
there is Recent Posts table in each log post. What to do with it? It will become stale as we add new posts. Or should it be like that so people browse what was happening around that time?

That's a good design question. Two philosophies:

**Keep it as "posts around that time"** — each post's sidebar shows the 5 most recent posts *at the time it was written*. Gives a sense of context, like a newspaper archive. But it's static and never needs updating since it reflects that moment in time. This is actually how old builder sites worked.

**Keep it as "latest 5 posts" site-wide** — always shows the newest content, helps readers navigate to recent activity. But then it needs updating every time you add a post (another thing `rebuild-site.py` would have to manage across all post files).

My recommendation: **remove it from individual posts entirely** and instead rely on the Build Log page for navigation. The prev/next links at the bottom of each post already let readers browse chronologically. The sidebar on posts is getting crowded anyway — less is more.

Alternatively, keep just the prev/next nav (which you already have) and drop the Recent Posts sidebar from posts.

What do you think?
lets do Keep it as "posts around that time" as it provides good context. For the < previous and next > give me some ideas

Good choice on keeping the contextual sidebar. For prev/next, here are some ideas:

**Option A — Plain arrows (current)**
```
← Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1        Back to Building! →
```

**Option B — Labeled with context**
```
‹ OLDER                                          NEWER ›
Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1        Back to Building!
```

**Option C — Newspaper style**
```
« Previously                              Up Next »
Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1        Back to Building!
```

**Option D — Date shown**
```
← Dec 1, 2021                              Feb 19, 2026 →
Horizontal Stabilizer: Update 1        Back to Building!
```

**Option E — Full card style** (slightly more visual)
```
┌─────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐
│ ← Previous entry    │    │   Next entry →       │
│ Horiz. Stab Upd. 1  │    │   Back to Building!  │
│ Dec 1, 2021         │    │   Feb 19, 2026        │
└─────────────────────┘    └─────────────────────┘
```

I'd lean toward **Option D** — the date adds real value on a build log since the time gaps between posts tell a story (e.g. a 4-year gap between posts is meaningful). Option E is the most polished but adds more HTML per post.

Which direction appeals to you?
I like option D. But then tell me the workflow. When we add a new post we do back to the last post then update its' html? How will we do the book keeping?

Good question. Right now prev/next is hardcoded in each post's HTML — so yes, every new post requires manually editing the previous post to add a "next" link. That's fragile. The clean solution: **let `rebuild-site.py` own prev/next**. Since it already reads all posts and knows their dates and paths, it can sort them chronologically and inject the prev/next nav into every post automatically. The workflow becomes: 1. Write new post HTML — **don't add prev/next links at all**, just leave a marker: ```html
``` 2. Run `python3 rebuild-site.py` 3. Script sorts all posts by date, finds each post's neighbors, and rewrites the `post-nav` div with the correct Option D style links and dates That's it. You never touch prev/next manually again. The only thing you need in each new post is the `build-date` meta tag (which you already need for the log anyway) — the script derives everything else from it. Want me to implement this?
yes

no wait, its a sliding window context. Its not the recent posts.

You're right — "Recent Posts" in the sidebar of a post should show the 5 posts closest in time to that post, not the 5 newest site-wide. So it gives the reader context of what was happening around that period of the build.

That's still something `rebuild-site.py` can own — for each post, find the 2 before and 2 after it chronologically (plus itself = 5), and inject that as the sidebar. Want me to do that?
wait if it is after posts as well then we will be updating past 3 posts everytime we add a new post, right?

Yes exactly — and that's fine because `rebuild-site.py` already rewrites all posts in one shot anyway (it already updates post-nav in all 13 posts every run). Adding Recent Posts to that same loop costs nothing extra. You just run the script once after adding a post and everything across all posts updates.

Want me to proceed?
how and why does it update all past posts from inception?

Because `update_post_nav` loops over every post in the `posts/` directory and rewrites the `post-nav` div in each one. It doesn't check if anything changed — it just rewrites all of them every time.

For post-nav that's necessary because adding post #14 changes post #13's "next" link — so #13 must be rewritten. But it also rewrites posts #1 through #12 even though nothing changed for them. It's slightly wasteful but harmless since it's just file writes on your local machine and takes milliseconds.

For Recent Posts (sliding window), same thing — adding post #14 shifts the window for posts #11, #12, #13. Posts further back are unaffected but the script would rewrite them anyway. Again harmless.

The alternative would be to only rewrite posts whose neighbors actually changed, but that's added complexity for no real benefit on a 13-post static site. Even at 500 posts it would still run in under a second.

Want me to proceed with the sliding window Recent Posts?

input.content: # Uran Khatola Factory — Website Static website chronicling the build of a Van's RV-8 homebuilt aircraft. **Live WordPress (source of truth for content):** https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/ **Owner:** Bibrak Qamar Chandio (bibrakc@gmail.com) ## Stack Pure HTML + CSS. No framework, no build step, no JavaScript. Host anywhere: GitHub Pages, Netlify, S3 static hosting, etc. ## Structure ``` website/ style.css # single stylesheet index.html # home page log.html # build log (by year + by category) about.html # about the builder and project rebuild-site.py # regenerates derived content — run after every new post posts/ 2021/ # one .html file per post, grouped by year 2022/ 2026/ img/ common/ # site-wide assets (banner, homepage photos, profile) 2021/ # per-post images, grouped by year/post-slug/ 2022/ 2026/ ``` ## Running Locally ```bash cd website python3 -m http.server 8080 # open http://localhost:8080 ``` ## Adding a New Post 1. Copy any existing post as a template (e.g. `posts/2026/attended-fiberglass-workshop.html`). 2. Save as `posts/YEAR/post-slug.html`. 3. Update: ``, `<h1>` inside `.post-header`, and body content. 4. Set the required meta tags in `<head>`: ```html <meta name="build-date" content="YYYY-MM-DD"> <meta name="build-category" content="Category Name"> <meta name="build-hours" content="X.X" data-category="category-key"> <!-- add data-bucket="other" if hours should NOT count toward build total --> ``` 5. Download post images into `img/YEAR/post-slug/` (see Media section below). 6. Add `<img>` tags in the post body. 7. Run `python3 rebuild-site.py` from the `website/` directory. That's it. The script handles: - Build hours table on `index.html` - By Category section and sidebar on `log.html` - Categories sidebar on `index.html` - "Around This Time" sidebar on every post - Prev/next navigation on every post ## Hours Categories | `data-category` key | Display label | Bucket | |----------------------|----------------------|---------| | `workshop` | Workshop | build | | `research-education` | Research & Education | build | | `empennage` | Empennage | build | | `wings` | Wings | build | | `fuselage` | Fuselage | build | | `firewall-forward` | Firewall Forward | build | | `finishing` | Finishing | build | | `social-flying` | Flying & Social | other | New categories auto-appear in the table. Add `data-bucket="other"` to exclude from build total. ## Media Workflow (importing from WordPress) ```bash # 1. Find image URLs in a WordPress post curl -s "https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/YEAR/MM/DD/post-slug/" \ | grep -oE 'https://urankhatolafactory\.wordpress\.com/wp-content/uploads/[^"? ]+\.(jpg|jpeg|png|gif|webp)' \ | sort -u # 2. Download into the post's image folder cd website/img/YEAR/post-slug/ curl -sO "https://...full-url-no-query-params..." # 3. Replace placeholder divs in the post HTML with real <img> tags ``` ## Design System | Token | Value | Usage | |----------------|------------|------------------------------| | `--bg` | `#ffffff` | Page background | | `--bg-alt` | `#f0f5f1` | Sidebar, intro boxes | | `--border` | `#b0a898` | Borders and dividers | | `--text` | `#1a1a1a` | Body text | | `--text-muted` | `#5a5a5a` | Dates, captions, meta | | `--accent` | `#8b1a1a` | Deep red — highlights | | `--link` | `#1a4a2e` | Link color | | `--nav-bg` | `#1a4a2e` | Nav, footer, sidebar headers | | `--max-width` | `860px` | Content max width | Fonts: **Courier New** (nav, meta, headers) + **Georgia** (body). Responsive: two-column grid, sidebar stacks below at ≤680px. ## Deployment (TODO) Not yet set up. Plan: GitHub Pages or Netlify. Use `rsync --checksum` to only upload changed files.

input.content:
# Agent Context — Uran Khatola Factory Website This file gives an AI agent everything it needs to work on this project without prior conversation history. ## Project Summary A static personal website for Bibrak Qamar Chandio, who is building a **Van's RV-8 homebuilt aircraft** in his garage in Cupertino, California. The site chronicles the build with blog-style posts, a build hours tracker, and links to social media. - **WordPress source:** https://urankhatolafactory.wordpress.com/ - **Owner/author:** Bibrak Qamar Chandio (`bibrakc@gmail.com`) - **Social:** YouTube, Facebook, Instagram (all `urankhatolafactory`) ## Tech Stack - Pure HTML5 + CSS3. No JavaScript, no framework, no build tool. - Single stylesheet: `website/style.css` - All pages are self-contained `.html` files. - `website/rebuild-site.py` — run after every new post to regenerate derived content. ## Design System | Token | Value | Usage | |---|---|---| | `--bg` | `#ffffff` | Page background (white) | | `--bg-alt` | `#f0f5f1` | Sidebar, intro boxes (light green tint) | | `--border` | `#b0a898` | All borders and dividers | | `--text` | `#1a1a1a` | Body text | | `--text-muted` | `#5a5a5a` | Dates, captions, meta | | `--accent` | `#8b1a1a` | Deep red — borders, hover, highlights | | `--link` | `#1a4a2e` | Link color | | `--nav-bg` | `#1a4a2e` | Nav, footer, sidebar headers (dark forest green) | | `--max-width` | `860px` | Content max width | **Fonts:** Courier New (nav, meta, headers) + Georgia serif (body). **Aesthetic:** Vintage 2000s homebuilder website. No animations, no gradients, flat layout. **Responsive:** Two-column grid (main + 220px sidebar). Sidebar stacks below main at ≤680px. **Header:** White background with banner image + site title in green. Red bottom border. ## File Map ``` website/ style.css index.html home — intro, 5 recent posts, hours sidebar, categories, links log.html build log — by year + by category (both on same page) about.html about Bibrak and the RV-8 project rebuild-site.py regenerates: hours table, categories, Around This Time, prev/next nav img/ common/ banner, homepage photos, profile photo 2021/post-slug/ per-post images 2022/post-slug/ 2026/post-slug/ posts/ 2021/ 10 posts 2022/ 1 post 2026/ 2 posts ``` ### All Posts (newest first) | File | Date | Category | |---|---|---| | posts/2026/attended-fiberglass-workshop.html | 2026-03-25 | Research & Education | | posts/2026/back-to-building.html | 2026-02-19 | Planning | | posts/2022/horizontal-stabilizer-update-2.html | 2022-03-19 | Empennage | | posts/2021/horizontal-stabilizer-update-1.html | 2021-12-01 | Empennage | | posts/2021/empennage-inventoried.html | 2021-10-27 | Empennage | | posts/2021/finished-the-practice-flap-kit.html | 2021-10-20 | Research & Education | | posts/2021/became-a-pilot-today.html | 2021-09-25 | Flying & Social | | posts/2021/a-beauty-came-to-kbmg.html | 2021-08-30 | Flying & Social | | posts/2021/marks-rv7.html | 2021-08-30 | Flying & Social | | posts/2021/getting-to-know-tools.html | 2021-07-11 | Planning | | posts/2021/update-on-empennage-crating-date.html | 2021-05-12 | Planning | | posts/2021/homebuilthelp-rv7-rudder-workshop-dvd.html | 2021-05-11 | Planning | | posts/2021/ordered-the-empennage-kit.html | 2021-04-26 | Planning | ## HTML Conventions ### Required meta tags in every post `` ```html ``` ### Post header block ```html

Post Title

📅 Month D, YYYY 📁 Category ⏱ X hrs logged
``` ### Image (real photo) ```html

Caption text.

``` ### Markers managed by rebuild-site.py (do not edit manually) ```html
``` ### Nav — mark the active page ```html
  • Build Log
    • ``` ### Path depths - Top-level pages (`index.html`, `log.html`, `about.html`): `style.css`, `img/common/...` - Posts (`posts/YEAR/post.html`): `../../style.css`, `../../img/...` ## How to Add a New Post 1. Copy `posts/2026/attended-fiberglass-workshop.html` as a template. 2. Save as `posts/YEAR/kebab-case-title.html`. 3. Update: ``, `<h1>`, post-meta (date, category, hours), body content. 4. Set the four meta tags (build-date, build-category, build-hours, optionally data-bucket). 5. Download images into `img/YEAR/post-slug/` and add `<img>` tags. 6. Leave `post-nav` and `recent-posts` divs as empty markers. 7. Run `python3 rebuild-site.py` from `website/`. 8. Add the post to the By Year section of `log.html` manually (the script handles By Category). 9. Update the 5 recent posts list on `index.html` if it's in the top 5. ## rebuild-site.py — What It Does Run: `python3 rebuild-site.py` from `website/` Scans all posts for meta tags and regenerates: 1. **Build Hours table** in `index.html` — sums `build-hours` by `data-category` 2. **Beyond the Build table** in `index.html` — categories with `data-bucket="other"` 3. **By Category section** in `log.html` — grouped by `build-category`, sorted newest first 4. **Categories sidebar** in `log.html` and `index.html` — counts per category with anchor links 5. **"Around This Time" sidebar** on every post — 2 newer + self + 2 older posts 6. **Prev/Next nav** on every post — with date and title, Option D style ## Hours Categories | `data-category` | Display label | Bucket | |---|---|---| | `workshop` | Workshop | build | | `research-education` | Research & Education | build | | `empennage` | Empennage | build | | `wings` | Wings | build | | `fuselage` | Fuselage | build | | `firewall-forward` | Firewall Forward | build | | `finishing` | Finishing | build | | `social-flying` | Flying & Social | other | New categories auto-appear. Labels auto-format from key if not in `CATEGORY_LABELS` dict. New `data-bucket="other"` categories auto-appear in "Beyond the Build" section. ## Current Build Hours (RV-8 only) | Category | Hours | |---|---| | Workshop | 4 | | Research & Education | 31 | | **Total** | **35** | | Flying & Social (beyond build) | 5.17 | ## External Links - Van's RV-8: https://www.vansaircraft.com/rv-8/ - EAA: https://www.eaa.org/ - HomebuiltHelp: https://homebuilthelp.com - YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCkw7kZ2sxUoKswTp_emBPEg - Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/urankhatolafactory - Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/urankhatolafactory/ - LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/bibrak-qamar-chandio-6444b51b/

New `data-bucket="other"` categories auto-appear in "Beyond the Build" section. ## Current Build Hours (RV-8 only) | Category | Hours | |---|---| | Workshop | 4 | | Research & Education | 31 | | **Total** | **35** | | Flying & Social (beyond build) | 5.17 | ## External Links - Van's RV-8: https://www.vansaircraft.com/rv-8/ - EAA: https://www.eaa.org/ - HomebuiltHelp: https://homebuilthelp.com - YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCkw7kZ2sxUoKswTp_emBPEg - Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/urankhatolafactory - Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/urankhatolafactory/ - LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/bibrak-qamar-chandio-6444b51b/

content.data:
5 copies. The `update_index` regex replaces from `

Build Hours

` through the end of that div, but the "Beyond the Build" section it injects is a *new* sibling div — so each run appends another one. Fix: widen the regex to consume any existing "Beyond the Build" div too.

def update_index(table_html): content = open(INDEX_HTML).read() # Replace Build Hours section + any immediately following Beyond the Build section new_content = re.sub( r'(

Build Hours

\s*).*?
' r'\s*' r'(\s*
\s*

Beyond the Build

.*?\s*
)?', lambda m: f'

Build Hours

\n {table_html}\n ', content, flags=re.DOTALL ) if new_content == content: print("No changes needed.") return open(INDEX_HTML, "w").write(new_content) print("index.html hours table updated.")

is there a way to see the page how it would be seen on a phone?

A few options:

**1. Browser DevTools (easiest, no extra tools)**  
Open the site in Chrome or Firefox, press `F12`, then click the phone/tablet icon (Toggle Device Toolbar). You can pick iPhone, Pixel, iPad etc. and see exactly how it renders.

**2. Firefox Responsive Design Mode**  
`F12` → click the phone icon, or `Ctrl+Shift+M`. Lets you drag to resize and pick device presets.

**3. Real device on same network**  
Start the local server:
```bash
cd website && python3 -m http.server 8080
```
Find your machine's local IP (`ip addr` or `hostname -I`), then open `http://192.168.x.x:8080` on your phone while on the same WiFi.

Option 3 is the most accurate since it's a real device with a real browser. Option 1/2 is faster for quick checks during development.

Which do you want to try?
